questions.specification: A plane wave strikes a boundary between air and water.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic C - Wave behaviour > C.3 Wave phenomena > C.3.1 Reflection, refraction, and diffraction
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.3.1 Reflection, refraction, and diffraction
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: All parts focus on refraction at an air–water boundary: defining refraction, changes in wave speed and wavelength in a new medium, and explaining bending due to a speed change across the boundary.

parts.content: Define the term “refraction”.
parts.markscheme:
The bending/change of direction of a wave as it passes from one medium to another (due to a change in speed) <marks>A1</marks>

[1 mark]

steps.title: Identify the wave behavior
steps.content:
This question asks us to define a specific wave phenomenon that occurs when a wave crosses a boundary. This topic is covered in **Topic C.3: Wave phenomena**. 

In this scenario, we have a plane wave moving from air into water. Because these two materials (media) have different properties, the wave will behave differently as it crosses the boundary.

highlights.text: A plane wave strikes a boundary between air and water.

steps.title: State the formal definition
steps.content:
Refraction is defined as the **bending** or **change in direction** of a wave as it passes from one medium into another. 

To earn the mark, you must mention both the change in direction and the fact that it happens when moving between media.

highlights.text: The bending/change of direction of a wave as it passes from one medium to another

steps.title: Explain the cause
steps.content:
A complete physics definition often includes the physical cause. The wave bends because its **speed changes** when it enters a new medium. 

For example, light travels slower in water than it does in air, which is what causes the "bending" effect you see when looking at a straw in a glass of water.

parts.content: State what happens to the speed of light as it enters water from air.
parts.markscheme:
Speed decreases <marks>A1</marks>

[1 mark]

steps.title: Identify the media
steps.content: The question asks us to describe the change in speed as light moves from **air** into **water**. To answer this, we need to compare how light behaves in these two different materials.

steps.title: Compare optical densities
steps.content:
In physics, we use the term **optical density** to describe how a medium affects the speed of light. Materials like water are more "optically dense" than air. 

This concept is a key part of **Topic C.3: Wave phenomena**, which explains how waves change behavior when crossing a boundary.

steps.title: Relate density to speed
steps.content: Using qualitative reasoning, we know that light travels at its maximum speed in a vacuum (and nearly that fast in air). When light enters a medium with a **higher optical density**, it encounters more interaction with the matter in that medium, which causes it to slow down.

steps.title: Determine the final result
steps.content:
Since water is more optically dense than air, the light cannot maintain its original speed. 

Therefore, the speed of light **decreases** as it enters the water.

steps.title: Recall the refractive index
steps.content:
To understand how speed changes, we look at the definition of the absolute refractive index ($n$). It is defined as the ratio of the speed of light in a vacuum ($c$) to the speed of light in the medium ($v$):

$$n = \frac{c}{v}$$

Rearranging this for speed, we get:

$$v = \frac{c}{n}$$

steps.title: Compare the two media
steps.content:
In IB Physics, we learn that water is **optically denser** than air. This means that the refractive index of water ($n_{\text{water}} \approx 1.33$) is greater than the refractive index of air ($n_{\text{air}} \approx 1.00$).

$$n_{\text{water}} > n_{\text{air}}$$

steps.title: Relate index to speed
steps.content:
Using our formula $v = \frac{c}{n}$, we can see that the speed of light is **inversely proportional** to the refractive index. 

Because light is moving from a medium with a lower refractive index (air) to a medium with a higher refractive index (water), the value of the denominator ($n$) increases, which causes the speed ($v$) to decrease.

gdc.instructions: Calculate the velocity in both media using v = c/n.

ti84.instructions: Divide the speed of light (3E8) by the refractive indices of air and water.

desmos.instructions: Evaluate the velocity for both refractive indices.

calculator.description: Compare the speed of light in air (n ≈ 1.00) vs water (n ≈ 1.33).

steps.title: State the final result
steps.content:
As the light enters the water, it slows down. Therefore, the speed decreases.

According to the markscheme, simply stating that the speed decreases earns the mark.

parts.content: Identify whether the wavelength increases or decreases in water.
parts.markscheme:
Wavelength decreases <marks>A1</marks>

[1 mark]

steps.title: Recall frequency invariance
steps.content:
When a wave travels from one medium to another, such as from air into water, its **frequency** ($f$) remains constant. The frequency is determined by the source of the wave and does not change when crossing a boundary.

This is a fundamental concept in **Topic C.3 Wave phenomena**.

steps.title: Analyze wave speed change
steps.content:
Water is optically denser than air. This means that the speed of light ($v$) **decreases** when it enters water from air. 

We can represent this change as:
$$v_{\text{water}} < v_{\text{air}}$$

steps.title: Apply the wave equation
steps.content:
From the **Waves** section of your data booklet, we have the wave equation:

$$v = f\lambda$$

To see how the wavelength ($\lambda$) changes, we can rearrange this formula for $\lambda$:

$$\lambda = \frac{v}{f}$$

steps.title: Determine wavelength change
steps.content:
Since the frequency ($f$) is constant and the speed ($v$) decreases, the wavelength ($\lambda$) must also decrease to satisfy the equation.

Mathematically, because $\lambda$ is directly proportional to $v$ when $f$ is constant:
$$\downarrow v \propto \downarrow \lambda$$

Therefore, the wavelength **decreases** in water.

steps.title: Recall the frequency principle
steps.content:
When a wave moves from one medium to another, such as from air to water, its **frequency ($f$) remains constant**. This is because the frequency is determined solely by the source of the wave. 

This principle is covered in **Topic C.2: Wave model**, and it is the foundation for understanding how wavelength and speed change during refraction.

steps.title: Connect index and wavelength
steps.content:
The refractive index ($n$) of a medium relates to how much the wave slows down and how its wavelength changes. Using the wave equation from the data booklet:

$$\text{Waves: } v = f\lambda$$

Since frequency is constant, the refractive index can be expressed as the ratio of the wavelengths in the two media:

$$n = \frac{\lambda_{\text{air}}}{\lambda_{\text{water}}}$$

steps.title: Compare refractive indices
steps.content:
Water is optically denser than air. While the refractive index of air is approximately $1.00$, the refractive index of water is roughly $1.33$. 

Crucially, for water, we know that $n > 1$.

steps.title: Determine wavelength change
steps.content:
Now, let's rearrange our relationship to solve for the wavelength in water:

$$\lambda_{\text{water}} = \frac{\lambda_{\text{air}}}{n}$$

Since we are dividing the air wavelength by a value of $n$ that is greater than $1$, the resulting value for $\lambda_{\text{water}}$ must be smaller than $\lambda_{\text{air}}$.

gdc.instructions: Calculate the ratio of wavelengths 1 / 1.33.

ti84.instructions: Divide 1 by 1.33 to see the ratio.

desmos.instructions: Enter the calculation to find the ratio.

calculator.description: If we assume a refractive index of 1.33 for water, we can see the scale factor by which the wavelength changes.

steps.title: State the final conclusion
steps.content:
Because the refractive index of water is higher than that of air, the wavelength must decrease to maintain the constant frequency of the wave.

Final Answer: **The wavelength decreases.**

steps.title: Compare optical density
steps.content:
To understand how the wave changes, we first compare the **optical density** of the two media. Water is more optically dense than air. 

In physics, a higher optical density means that the wave will travel more slowly through that medium. This is related to the refractive index $n$ found in **Topic C.3 (Wave phenomena)**.

highlights.text: strikes a boundary between air and water

steps.title: Analyze wave speed
steps.content:
When the plane wave moves from air into the more optically dense water, its speed decreases. 

Imagine the wavefronts (the crests of the wave) hitting the water. As the leading edge of the wave enters the water, it immediately slows down, while the part of the wave still in the air continues at its original higher speed.

steps.title: Explain wavefront compression
steps.content:
Because the front of the wave has slowed down while the back is still catching up at a higher speed, the wavefronts begin to 'bunch up.' 

This physical 'bunching' is known as **wavefront compression**. Since the frequency $f$ (the number of waves arriving per second) must stay constant at the boundary, the physical distance between these wavefronts must change.

steps.title: Determine wavelength change
steps.content:
The distance between two consecutive wavefronts is defined as the **wavelength** ($\lambda$). 

Since the wavefronts have 'bunched up' due to the decrease in speed, the distance between them is now smaller. We can see this relationship in the wave equation from the **Waves** section of your data booklet:

$$v = f\lambda$$

If the frequency $f$ is constant and the speed $v$ decreases, the wavelength $\lambda$ must also decrease to maintain the equality.

steps.title: Final Conclusion
steps.content:
Based on the conceptual argument of wavefront compression in a more optically dense medium, the wavelength decreases.

In an IB exam, simply stating the direction of change is enough for this 1-mark question.

parts.content: Suggest why the wave changes direction at the boundary.
parts.markscheme:
Wave slows down in water <marks>A1</marks>
Causes the wavefront to bend / follows Snell’s Law <marks>A1</marks>

[2 marks]

steps.title: Identify the process
steps.content: When a wave passes from one medium (air) to another (water), it undergoes a process called **refraction**. This process is fundamentally caused by a change in the wave's velocity as it enters the new medium.

steps.title: Analyze speed change
steps.content:
In this scenario, the wave transitions from air into water. Water is more dense than air, which causes the wave to **slow down**. 

According to the markscheme, identifying this change in speed is essential for the first mark.

steps.title: Explain the bending
steps.content: As the plane wave hits the boundary at an angle, different parts of the wavefront reach the water at different times. The part of the wave that enters the water first slows down immediately, while the part still in the air continues at its original higher speed. This difference in speed across the wavefront causes the path of the wave to **bend towards the normal**.

steps.title: Reference Snell's Law
steps.content:
This relationship between the change in speed and the change in direction is mathematically described by **Snell’s Law** found in your data booklet:

$$n_{1}\sin\theta_{1}=n_{2}\sin\theta_{2}$$

Where $n$ is the refractive index (which is inversely proportional to the speed of the wave in that medium). A decrease in speed leads to a decrease in the angle relative to the normal, explaining the change in direction.

steps.title: Identify the speed change
steps.content:
The first thing to understand is that the speed of a wave depends on the medium it is traveling through. When a plane wave moves from air into water, it slows down because water is more optically dense than air.

This fundamental change in speed is the root cause of refraction. This concept is found in **Topic C.3: Wave phenomena**.

steps.title: Analyze the wavefront timing
steps.content:
Imagine a plane wavefront (a straight line representing the crest of the wave) hitting the boundary at an angle. Because of this angle, different parts of the wavefront reach the water at different times.

The part of the wavefront that hits the water first will start to slow down immediately, while the part still in the air continues to move at its original, faster speed.

steps.title: The pivot effect
steps.content:
Since one side of the wavefront is moving slower than the other for a brief moment, the wavefront 'pivots' or 'swings' around. 

In the time it takes the 'fast' end (still in air) to reach the boundary, the 'slow' end (already in water) has traveled a shorter distance. This difference in distance traveled causes the wavefront to change its orientation, resulting in a change of direction.

steps.title: Relate to Snell's Law
steps.content:
This physical 'bending' of the wavefront is what we describe mathematically using **Snell's Law** from your data booklet:

$$n_{1}\sin\theta_{1}=n_{2}\sin\theta_{2}$$

Because the wave slows down in the second medium, the angle of refraction $\theta_2$ becomes smaller than the angle of incidence $\theta_1$, meaning the wave bends toward the normal.

highlights.text: Causes the wavefront to bend / follows Snell’s Law

questions.specification: A string of length $L = 0.80\ \text{m}$ is fixed at both ends and supports a standing wave pattern with three antinodes when under tension. The wave speed on the string is $v = 120\ \text{m/s}$.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic C - Wave behaviour > C.4 Standing waves and resonance > C.4.2 Standing wave patterns in systems
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.4.2 Standing wave patterns in systems
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question is specifically about a string fixed at both ends with three antinodes, requiring identification of harmonic number, wavelength, frequency, and sketching node/antinode pattern—i.e., standing-wave modes in a bounded system.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic C - Wave behaviour > C.4 Standing waves and resonance > C.4.1 Formation and properties of standing waves
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.4.1 Formation and properties of standing waves
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Uses core standing-wave relationships (L = nλ/2, f = v/λ) and asks how changing tension affects frequency via wave speed, which relies on properties/formation of standing waves.

parts.content: State the harmonic number of this standing wave.
parts.markscheme:
- **State** that for a standing wave with three antinodes fixed at both ends, it is the third harmonic ($n = 3$) <marks>A1</marks>

1 mark total

steps.title: Identify boundary conditions
steps.content:
In this problem, we are looking at a string that is **fixed at both ends**. This is a crucial piece of information because the relationship between the wave pattern and the harmonic number depends on these boundary conditions.

You can learn more about these patterns in **Topic C.4: Standing waves and resonance**.

steps.title: Apply antinode correspondence
steps.content:
For a string fixed at both ends, there is a direct relationship between the standing wave pattern and the harmonic number $n$:

$$\text{Number of antinodes} = n$$

In the first harmonic ($n=1$), there is 1 antinode; in the second harmonic ($n=2$), there are 2 antinodes, and so on.

steps.title: Determine harmonic number
steps.content: The question specifies that the standing wave supports **three antinodes**. Using the direct correspondence rule for fixed strings, this means we are looking at the third harmonic ($n = 3$).

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The harmonic number of this standing wave is $n = 3$. 

In an IB exam, simply stating the correct value based on the number of antinodes is sufficient for the mark.

steps.title: Analyze the wave pattern
steps.content:
In this problem, we are looking at a standing wave on a string fixed at both ends. For this configuration, the ends must be nodes (points of no displacement). 

The problem states there are **three antinodes** (points of maximum displacement) in the pattern. This concept is part of **Topic C.4: Standing waves and resonance**.

steps.title: Link antinodes to loops
steps.content:
Using the **Standing Wave Loop Analysis** approach, we visualize the wave as a series of "loops." On a string fixed at both ends, each loop contains exactly one antinode. 

Since there are three antinodes, there must be three loops (three half-wavelength segments) making up the standing wave pattern.

steps.title: State the harmonic number
steps.content:
For a string fixed at both ends, the harmonic number $n$ is simply equal to the number of loops (or the number of antinodes) present in the standing wave.

$$\begin{aligned} n &= \text{number of antinodes} \\ n &= 3 \end{aligned}$$

Therefore, this standing wave is the third harmonic.

parts.content: Calculate the wavelength of the wave on the string.
parts.markscheme:
- **Identify** the relationship between string length and wavelength for the third harmonic: $L = \frac{3\lambda}{2}$ or $\lambda = \frac{2L}{3}$ <marks>M1</marks>
- **Calculate** the final value: $\lambda = \frac{2 \times 0.80}{3} = 0.533\ \text{m}$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

parts.content: Calculate the frequency of the wave.
parts.markscheme:
- **Calculate** the frequency using the wave equation: $f = \frac{v}{\lambda} = \frac{120}{0.533} = 225\ \text{Hz}$ <marks>A1</marks>

1 mark total

steps.title: Analyze the wave pattern
steps.content:
In a standing wave on a string fixed at both ends, the number of antinodes tells us which harmonic we are looking at. Each antinode represents one "loop," which is half a wavelength ($\frac{\lambda}{2}$).

Since the question states there are three antinodes, the total length of the string ($L$) must equal three half-wavelengths:

$$L = 3 \left( \frac{\lambda}{2} \right) = \frac{3\lambda}{2}$$

steps.title: Calculate the wavelength
steps.content:
Now we can solve for the wavelength ($\lambda$) by substituting the given length $L = 0.80\ \text{m}$ into our relationship:

$$\begin{aligned} 0.80 &= \frac{3\lambda}{2} \\ \lambda &= \frac{2 \times 0.80}{3} \\ \lambda &\approx 0.533\ \text{m} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Evaluate (2 * 0.80) / 3.

ti84.instructions: Enter (2 * 0.80) / 3 and press ENTER.

desmos.instructions: Type (2 * 0.8) / 3 into a calculation cell.

calculator.description: Calculate the wavelength by multiplying the length by 2 and dividing by 3.

steps.title: Calculate the frequency
steps.content:
Finally, we use the wave equation from the data booklet to find the frequency ($f$). Given the wave speed $v = 120\ \text{m/s}$:

$$\begin{aligned} v &= f\lambda \\ f &= \frac{v}{\lambda} \\ f &= \frac{120}{0.533...} \\ f &= 225\ \text{Hz} \end{aligned}$$

This calculation aligns with the 1 mark available for correctly applying the wave equation.

gdc.instructions: Divide the speed by the previously calculated wavelength.

ti84.instructions: Enter 120 / (Ans) or 120 / (1.6 / 3) and press ENTER.

desmos.instructions: Evaluate 120 / (1.6 / 3).

calculator.description: Calculate the frequency using the exact value of wavelength for precision.

steps.title: Identify the harmonic number
steps.content:
For a string fixed at both ends, the number of antinodes in a standing wave pattern corresponds directly to the harmonic number ($n$). Since the question specifies the pattern has three antinodes, we are looking for the frequency of the third harmonic, so $n = 3$. 

This concept is part of **Topic C.4: Standing waves and resonance**.

steps.title: Calculate fundamental frequency
steps.content:
The fundamental frequency ($f_1$) is the lowest possible frequency for a standing wave on this string. For a string of length $L$ fixed at both ends, the wavelength of the fundamental is $\lambda_1 = 2L$. 

Using the wave equation from the data booklet:

$$v = f\lambda$$

We can rearrange this to find the fundamental frequency:

$$f_1 = \frac{v}{2L}$$

Substituting the given values:

$$f_1 = \frac{120}{2 \times 0.80}$$

$$f_1 = 75\text{ Hz}$$

gdc.instructions: Evaluate 120 divided by the quantity 2 times 0.80.

ti84.instructions: Enter 120 / (2 * 0.80) and press ENTER.

desmos.instructions: Type 120 / (2 * 0.80) into a new expression line.

calculator.description: Calculate the fundamental frequency (f1).

steps.title: Scale to third harmonic
steps.content:
In a system fixed at both ends, higher harmonic frequencies are integer multiples of the fundamental frequency ($f_n = n \times f_1$). 

To find the frequency for the pattern with three antinodes ($n = 3$):

$$\begin{aligned} f_3 &= 3 \times f_1 \\ f_3 &= 3 \times 75 \\ f_3 &= 225\text{ Hz} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Multiply 75 by 3.

ti84.instructions: Enter 75 * 3 and press ENTER.

desmos.instructions: Type 75 * 3 into a new expression line.

calculator.description: Multiply the fundamental frequency by 3 to find the third harmonic.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The frequency of the wave is $225\text{ Hz}$. 

In an IB exam, you earn the mark for the correct final value with units. While the markscheme uses the wavelength method, this fundamental scaling method is a perfectly valid and often faster way to reach the same result.

parts.content: Sketch the standing wave pattern on the string, showing all nodes and antinodes.
parts.markscheme:
- **Sketch** shows 3 loops with 3 antinodes (A) and 4 nodes (N), including the fixed ends <marks>A1</marks>
- **Verify** correct spacing and sinusoidal shape along the length of the $0.80\ \text{m}$ string <marks>A1</marks>

Example sketch:
![Standing wave with 3 loops](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/plots/a594676a-9094-4639-a3f1-739dc620225c.png)

2 marks total

parts.content:
The tension in the string is increased.

Explain the effect this has on the frequency of the standing wave, assuming the harmonic number stays the same.
parts.markscheme:
- **Identify** that increasing tension increases the wave speed $v$ (using $v = \sqrt{\frac{T}{\mu}}$) <marks>R1</marks>
- **Conclude** that since $f = \frac{v}{\lambda}$ and the wavelength $\lambda$ is constant (fixed by string length and harmonic number), the frequency increases <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Relate tension and speed
steps.content:
The speed of a wave on a string depends on the tension $T$ and the mass per unit length $\mu$. The relationship is given by:

$$v = \sqrt{\frac{T}{\mu}}$$

When the tension is increased, the wave speed $v$ increases proportionally to the square root of the tension.

highlights.text: The tension in the string is increased.

steps.title: Identify constant wavelength
steps.content:
For a standing wave on a string fixed at both ends, the wavelength $\lambda$ is determined by the string length $L$ and the harmonic number $n$ (the number of antinodes). The formula is:

$$\lambda = \frac{2L}{n}$$

Since both the length $L$ and the harmonic number $n$ remain constant, the wavelength $\lambda$ of the standing wave does not change.

steps.title: Determine frequency change
steps.content:
We can now use the wave equation from the data booklet to relate speed, frequency, and wavelength:

$$v = f\lambda$$

Rearranging for frequency, we get:

$$f = \frac{v}{\lambda}$$

Because the wave speed $v$ has increased and the wavelength $\lambda$ remains constant, the frequency $f$ of the standing wave must increase.

steps.title: Summary of marks
steps.content:
To secure both marks on this question:
1. **State** that increasing tension increases the wave speed $v$ (using the relationship $v \propto \sqrt{T}$).
2. **Explain** that because the wavelength $\lambda$ is fixed by the physical setup, the increase in speed results in an increase in frequency $f$.

steps.title: Start with wave speed
steps.content:
To understand how frequency changes, we first look at the wave speed equation from the **Waves** section of your data booklet:

$$v = f\lambda$$

Rearranging this for frequency $f$, we get:

$$f = \frac{v}{\lambda}$$

steps.title: Identify tension relationship
steps.content:
For a string, the wave speed $v$ is determined by the tension $T$ and the linear mass density $\mu$ (mass per unit length). The relationship is:

$$v = \sqrt{\frac{T}{\mu}}$$

This shows that the speed of the wave is directly proportional to the square root of the tension.

highlights.text: tension in the string is increased

steps.title: Derive the combined formula
steps.content:
By substituting the expression for $v$ into our frequency equation, we can create a single expression that relates frequency directly to tension:

$$\begin{aligned} f &= \frac{1}{\lambda} \cdot v \\ f &= \frac{1}{\lambda} \sqrt{\frac{T}{\mu}} \end{aligned}$$

This formula reveals that frequency $f$ is proportional to the square root of tension $\sqrt{T}$, provided that the wavelength $\lambda$ and mass density $\mu$ remain constant.

steps.title: Check for constant variables
steps.content:
Since the string length $L$ is fixed and the "harmonic number stays the same" (three antinodes), the wavelength $\lambda$ does not change. 

$$\lambda = \frac{2L}{n} = \frac{2(0.80)}{3} \approx 0.533\text{ m}$$

Because $\lambda$ and $\mu$ are constant, we can see from our derived formula that $f \propto \sqrt{T}$.

steps.title: State the final effect
steps.content:
As the tension $T$ increases, the wave speed $v$ increases. Since the wavelength $\lambda$ is fixed by the string's geometry, the frequency $f$ must also increase to maintain the relationship $v = f\lambda$.

In short: **The frequency of the standing wave increases.**

questions.specification: A diffraction grating with 5000 lines/cm is used to measure the wavelength of light.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic C - Wave behaviour > C.3 Wave phenomena > C.3.3 Diffraction and interference patterns (HL only)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.3.3 Diffraction and interference patterns (HL only)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Uses a diffraction grating (line density to slit spacing) and grating equation to find wavelength and maximum observable order, which is diffraction/interference pattern analysis.

parts.content: Calculate the slit separation in metres.
parts.markscheme:
$$ d = \frac{1}{5000 \times 100} = 2.00 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m} $$
<marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Convert density to SI units
steps.content:
The question gives the line density of the diffraction grating as $5000$ lines per centimeter. To work in standard SI units (meters), we first convert this to lines per meter. Since there are $100$ centimeters in $1$ meter, we multiply by $100$:

$$\text{Lines per meter} = 5000 \times 100 = 500,000 \text{ lines/m}$$

This represents the total number of slits packed into every meter of the grating.

steps.title: Calculate slit separation d
steps.content:
The slit separation, $d$, is the physical distance between the centers of two adjacent slits. It is defined as the reciprocal of the line density in meters:

$$d = \frac{1}{\text{lines per meter}}$$

Substituting our value:

$$d = \frac{1}{500,000}$$

gdc.instructions: Enter 1 / 500000. Use the S-D key if necessary to convert the fraction to a decimal.

ti84.instructions: Enter 1 divided by 500,000 and press ENTER. To see the result in scientific notation, press MODE, select SCI, and press ENTER.

desmos.instructions: Type 1/500000 into the input bar.

calculator.description: Calculate the reciprocal of the line density to find the slit separation.

steps.title: Final answer in meters
steps.content:
Performing the division, we get the final value for the slit separation. It is standard practice in IB Physics to express such small values in scientific notation.

$$d = 2.00 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}$$

This calculation earns **1 mark** for the correct final value with units.

steps.title: Define the Slit Separation
steps.content:
In physics, the slit separation $d$ of a diffraction grating is the distance between the centers of two adjacent lines. Since we are given the number of lines per centimeter ($N$), we can find $d$ by taking the reciprocal of this value. This concept is covered in **Topic C.3: Wave phenomena**.

$$d = \frac{1}{N}$$

steps.title: Calculate Separation in cm
steps.content:
First, we calculate the separation in centimeters by taking the reciprocal of the given line density:

$$d_{\text{cm}} = \frac{1}{5000} \text{ cm}$$

gdc.instructions: Enter 1/5000 in the calculation screen.

ti84.instructions: Press 1, then the divide key, then 5000, and press ENTER.

desmos.instructions: Type 1/5000 into the input bar.

calculator.description: Calculate the reciprocal of 5000 to find the separation in centimeters.

steps.title: Convert Result to Meters
steps.content:
The question asks for the answer in metres. Since there are $100$ centimeters in $1$ meter, we convert our result by dividing by $100$:

$$\begin{aligned} d &= \frac{0.0002}{100} \text{ m} \\ d &= 2.00 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Enter 0.0002 / 100 in the calculation screen.

ti84.instructions: Press 0.0002, then the divide key, then 100, and press ENTER.

desmos.instructions: Type 0.0002 / 100 into the input bar.

calculator.description: Convert the value from cm to m by dividing by 100.

highlights.text: Calculate the slit separation in metres.

steps.title: Identify the given information
steps.content: The question provides the density of lines on a diffraction grating. We know there are 5000 lines packed into every single centimeter. Our goal is to find the distance between these lines (the slit separation, $d$) and express it in meters.

steps.title: Convert length to meters
steps.content:
To get our final answer in meters, it's easiest to convert our reference length from centimeters to meters right away. 

Since $100\text{ cm} = 1\text{ m}$, we can find the length of $1\text{ cm}$ in meters:

$$1\text{ cm} = \frac{1}{100}\text{ m} = 0.01\text{ m}$$

gdc.instructions: Divide 1 by 100.

ti84.instructions: Divide 1 by 100 on the main screen.

desmos.instructions: Type 1/100 into the expression bar.

calculator.description: Convert 1 cm to meters.

steps.title: Calculate separation using ratio
steps.content:
Using the **Direct Ratio Approach**, we know that $5000$ slits are contained within a total distance of $0.01\text{ m}$. To find the separation of just one slit ($d$), we divide that total distance by the number of lines:

$$d = \frac{\text{Total distance in meters}}{\text{Number of lines}}$$

$$d = \frac{0.01\text{ m}}{5000}$$

gdc.instructions: Divide 0.01 by 5000.

ti84.instructions: Divide 0.01 by 5000. To see the result in scientific notation, press [MODE] and select 'SCI' before calculating.

desmos.instructions: Type 0.01 / 5000 to find the result.

calculator.description: Calculate the slit separation.

steps.title: Finalize the result
steps.content:
Performing the calculation gives us the slit separation in meters. 

$$d = 2.00 \times 10^{-6}\text{ m}$$

This small distance is typical for diffraction gratings used in the physics lab to study visible light! Note that in the IB exam, the mark is awarded for the correct substitution and final value with units.

parts.content: Light produces a second-order maximum at $42^\circ$. Calculate the wavelength.
parts.markscheme:
Substitute into $n\lambda = d\sin\theta$:
$$ \lambda = \frac{2.00 \times 10^{-6} \times \sin 42^\circ}{2} $$
<marks>1 mark</marks>
$$ \lambda = 6.69 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m} $$
<marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Calculate grating spacing d
steps.content:
First, we need to find the grating spacing, $d$, which is the distance between adjacent lines on the grating. We are given the line density as 5000 lines per centimeter. 

To find $d$ in meters, we take the reciprocal of the line density and convert centimeters to meters:

$$d = \frac{1}{5000 \text{ lines/cm}} = 0.0002 \text{ cm}$$

Converting to meters ($1 \text{ m} = 100 \text{ cm}$):

$$d = \frac{0.0002}{100} = 2.00 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate 1 / 500000

calculator.description: Calculate the grating spacing d in meters.

steps.title: Apply diffraction grating equation
steps.content:
For a diffraction grating, the maxima occur at angles $\theta$ that satisfy the equation:

$$n\lambda = d\sin\theta$$

From the question, we know:
- Order of the maximum, $n = 2$
- Angle, $\theta = 42^\circ$
- Grating spacing, $d = 2.00 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}$

Rearranging to solve for the wavelength, $\lambda$:

$$\lambda = \frac{d\sin\theta}{n}$$

steps.title: Calculate the final wavelength
steps.content:
Now, substitute the values into the formula to find $\lambda$. This is where you would earn your first mark in an IB exam.

$$\lambda = \frac{2.00 \times 10^{-6} \times \sin(42^\circ)}{2}$$

Evaluating this gives:

$$\lambda = 6.69 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}$$

You can also express this as $669 \text{ nm}$.

gdc.instructions: Set angle units to degrees. Calculate (2e-6 * sin(42)) / 2

ti84.instructions: Ensure MODE is set to DEGREE. Enter (2E-6 * sin(42)) / 2

desmos.instructions: Switch to Degree mode in settings. Type (2 * 10^-6 * sin(42)) / 2

calculator.description: Calculate the wavelength. Ensure your calculator is in DEGREE mode.

parts.content: Determine the maximum possible order that could be observed.
parts.markscheme:
$$ n_{\text{max}} = \frac{d}{\lambda} = \frac{2.00 \times 10^{-6}}{6.69 \times 10^{-7}} \approx 2.99 $$
Maximum order $= 2$
<marks>1 mark</marks>

questions.specification: A pipe open at both ends produces a standing sound wave.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic C - Wave behaviour > C.4 Standing waves and resonance > C.4.2 Standing wave patterns in systems
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.4.2 Standing wave patterns in systems
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Open–open pipe standing-wave modes involve specific boundary conditions and patterns (nodes/antinodes), harmonics, and relating pipe length to wavelength.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic C - Wave behaviour > C.4 Standing waves and resonance > C.4.1 Formation and properties of standing waves
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.4.1 Formation and properties of standing waves
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Parts ask for fundamental mode sketch, adjacent node spacing, and using v=fλ—core properties/relationships of standing waves.

parts.content: Draw the fundamental mode.
parts.markscheme:
- Correct shape showing antinodes at both ends and one node in the middle <marks>A1</marks>
- Correct labeling of node (N) and antinodes (A) <marks>A1</marks>
![Fundamental mode standing wave in open pipe](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/plots/2d40f007-ae30-467e-82a3-b354f647905c.png)

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify boundary conditions
steps.content: In a pipe that is **open at both ends**, the air molecules at the openings are free to move with maximum amplitude. This means that both ends of the pipe must be **displacement antinodes** ($A$). This is a key concept from **Topic C.4 Standing waves and resonance**.

steps.title: Determine fundamental mode shape
steps.content:
The **fundamental mode** (or first harmonic) is the simplest possible standing wave pattern that satisfies these boundary conditions. To have an antinode at each end, the simplest pattern must have exactly **one node ($N$)** in the center of the pipe.

The displacement envelope shows the extent of the air molecules' vibration. It looks like two cones meeting at a single point in the middle.

steps.title: Draw and label
steps.content:
When drawing your diagram, ensure you show the following for the full 2 marks:

1.  **The Shape:** Draw two curved lines (the envelope) that are furthest apart at the ends and cross at a single point in the center.
2.  **The Labels:** Clearly mark the ends as **A** (Antinodes) and the center crossing point as **N** (Node).

$$\text{A} \longleftrightarrow \text{N} \longleftrightarrow \text{A}$$

Your final diagram should look like this:
![Fundamental mode standing wave in open pipe](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/plots/2d40f007-ae30-467e-82a3-b354f647905c.png)

parts.content: State the distance between adjacent nodes.
parts.markscheme:
- State the distance: Half a wavelength ($\dfrac{\lambda}{2}$) <marks>A1</marks>

1 mark total

steps.title: Define standing wave nodes
steps.content: In a standing wave, **nodes** are positions where the amplitude of the oscillation is always zero. These occur because of the destructive interference between two waves of the same frequency traveling in opposite directions. This concept is covered in **Topic C.4: Standing waves and resonance**.

steps.title: Apply standing wave properties
steps.content:
A fundamental property of all standing waves is that the distance between any two consecutive (adjacent) nodes is exactly **half a wavelength**. 

Mathematically, if the wavelength of the sound wave is represented by $\lambda$, the distance $d$ between adjacent nodes is:

$$d = \frac{\lambda}{2}$$

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content: The distance between adjacent nodes is half a wavelength, or $\frac{\lambda}{2}$. This is a standard property you are expected to recall for IB Physics exams.

steps.title: Recall boundary conditions
steps.content: In physics, we describe standing waves in pipes based on their 'boundary conditions'. For a pipe **open at both ends**, the air molecules are free to move at the exits, which creates **displacement antinodes** at each end. Between these antinodes, there must be at least one node where the air displacement is zero.

steps.title: Sketch the second harmonic
steps.content:
To visualize the distance between nodes, let's look at the **second harmonic** (also called the first overtone). In an open-open pipe, this harmonic contains two nodes. The pattern of displacement from one end to the other looks like this:

$$\text{Antinode (A)} \rightarrow \text{Node (N)} \rightarrow \text{Antinode (A)} \rightarrow \text{Node (N)} \rightarrow \text{Antinode (A)}$$

This entire pattern fits within the length of the pipe and represents exactly **one full wavelength** ($\lambda$).

steps.title: Measure the nodal distance
steps.content:
Let's break down the distances in our second harmonic sketch ($A \text{-} N \text{-} A \text{-} N \text{-} A$):

- The distance from an antinode to the next node is $\frac{\lambda}{4}$.
- The distance from a node to the next antinode is $\frac{\lambda}{4}$.

To find the distance between the two **adjacent nodes** ($N \text{ to } N$), we add these segments together:

$$\text{Distance}_{N \to N} = (N \to A) + (A \to N) = \frac{\lambda}{4} + \frac{\lambda}{4} = \frac{2\lambda}{4} = \frac{\lambda}{2}$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate 0.25 + 0.25.

ti84.instructions: Enter 1/4 + 1/4 and convert to a fraction.

desmos.instructions: Add the two fractions.

calculator.description: Verify the sum of two quarters equals one half.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
Regardless of which harmonic we choose, the repeating pattern of a standing wave ensures that the distance between any two consecutive nodes is always half of the wavelength.

$$\text{Distance} = \frac{\lambda}{2}$$

parts.content: If the pipe is 0.85 m long, calculate the wavelength of the first harmonic.
parts.markscheme:
- Substitute $L=0.85$ into $\lambda = 2L$: $\lambda = 2(0.85) = 1.70 \text{ m}$ <marks>A1</marks>

1 mark total

parts.content: Calculate the fundamental frequency if the speed of sound is 340 m/s.
parts.markscheme:
- Calculate the frequency: $f = \dfrac{v}{\lambda} = \dfrac{340}{1.70} = 200 \text{ Hz}$ <marks>A1</marks>

1 mark total

steps.title: Analyze the standing wave pattern
steps.content:
For a pipe open at both ends, the fundamental mode (the first harmonic) consists of displacement antinodes at both ends and a single node in the middle. This specific pattern means the length of the pipe $L$ corresponds to exactly half of a wavelength. This concept is part of **Topic C.4 Standing waves and resonance**.

$$L = \frac{\lambda}{2}$$

steps.title: Determine the wavelength
steps.content:
Using the relationship for a pipe open at both ends, we can rearrange to find the wavelength $\lambda$. Given that the pipe length is $0.85\text{ m}$, we calculate:

$$\lambda = 2L = 2 \times 0.85 = 1.70\text{ m}$$

steps.title: Select the wave equation
steps.content: To find the frequency, we use the wave equation found in the **Waves** section of your data booklet. This equation relates the speed of the wave, its frequency, and its wavelength.

steps.title: Calculate the fundamental frequency
steps.content:
We now rearrange the wave equation to solve for frequency ($f$) and substitute the given speed of sound and the wavelength we just found:

$$\begin{aligned} f &= \frac{v}{\lambda} \\ f &= \frac{340}{1.70} \\ f &= 200\text{ Hz} \end{aligned}$$

This matches the markscheme requirement for the final calculation.

gdc.instructions: In the Run-Matrix menu, perform the division 340 ÷ 1.70.

ti84.instructions: Enter 340 divided by 1.70 on the main screen.

desmos.instructions: Type the expression into the input field.

calculator.description: Verify the frequency calculation.

steps.title: Identify the harmonic formula
steps.content:
For a pipe open at both ends, the frequency of the $n$-th harmonic can be calculated using the direct harmonic formula:

$$f_n = \frac{nv}{2L}$$

Where:
- $n$ is the harmonic number ($n = 1$ for the fundamental frequency)
- $v$ is the speed of sound
- $L$ is the length of the pipe

For the fundamental mode, the wavelength $\lambda$ is equal to $2L$. Therefore, the formula simplifies to the wave equation found in the **Topic C.2** section of your data booklet:

$$v = f\lambda \implies f = \frac{v}{\lambda}$$

steps.title: Substitute the given values
steps.content:
From the question, we know the speed of sound is $340 \text{ m/s}$. From previous parts of this problem, we have determined that the wavelength $\lambda$ for this standing wave is $1.70 \text{ m}$.

We substitute these values into our rearranged equation:

$$f = \frac{340}{1.70}$$

steps.title: Calculate the final result
steps.content:
Performing the division gives us the fundamental frequency in Hertz (Hz).

$$f = 200 \text{ Hz}$$

This matches the markscheme requirement for the **A1** mark.

gdc.instructions: In the Run-Matrix menu, enter 340 / 1.70.

ti84.instructions: Enter 340 divided by 1.70 and press ENTER.

desmos.instructions: Type 340 / 1.70 into the input bar.

calculator.description: Calculate the fundamental frequency by dividing the speed of sound by the wavelength.

questions.specification: A ray of light enters a transparent block from air at an angle of incidence of $30^\circ$.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic C - Wave behaviour > C.3 Wave phenomena > C.3.1 Reflection, refraction, and diffraction
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.3.1 Reflection, refraction, and diffraction
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question is about light refraction at an interface, defining refractive index, stating Snell’s law, and calculating an angle of refraction—core content of reflection/refraction.

parts.content: Define the term “refractive index”.
parts.markscheme:
Ratio of speed of light in vacuum to speed in the medium.
<marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Identify the concept
steps.content: To define the **refractive index**, we look at how the speed of light changes as it moves from one medium (like a vacuum) into another (like glass or water). This is covered in **Topic C.3: Wave phenomena**.

steps.title: State the ratio
steps.content:
Using the **Ratio of Speeds** approach, the absolute refractive index ($n$) of a material is defined as the ratio of the speed of light in a vacuum to the speed of light in that material.

Mathematically, this is expressed as:

$$n = \frac{c}{v}$$

Where:
*   $c$ is the speed of light in a vacuum (approximately $3.00 \times 10^8 \text{ m s}^{-1}$)
*   $v$ is the speed of light in the medium

steps.title: Final definition
steps.content:
To earn the mark, you must state the definition clearly:

**Refractive index is the ratio of the speed of light in a vacuum to the speed of light in the medium.**

steps.title: Identify the relationship
steps.content:
To define the **refractive index**, we start by looking at how light behaves as it moves from one medium to another. In your IB Data Booklet, this is described by **Snell's Law** in the Waves section.

Snell's Law relates the angles of the light rays to the refractive indices of the two materials.

steps.title: Apply Ratio of Sines
steps.content:
Using the **Ratio of Sines** approach, we consider light entering a medium from a vacuum or air (where the refractive index $n_1 \approx 1$). 

In this specific case, the refractive index ($n$) of the second medium is defined as the ratio of the sine of the **angle of incidence** ($\theta_1$) to the sine of the **angle of refraction** ($\theta_2$):

$$n = \frac{\sin \theta_1}{\sin \theta_2}$$

steps.title: State the official definition
steps.content:
While the ratio of sines is a valid way to calculate the refractive index, the formal definition required for marks in the IB Physics curriculum is based on the **speed of light**. 

The refractive index is the ratio of the **speed of light in a vacuum** ($c$) to the **speed of light in the medium** ($v$):

$$n = \frac{c}{v}$$

To ensure you get the mark, always use this speed-based definition.

parts.content: State the equation that relates angle of incidence and refraction.
parts.markscheme:
$n = \dfrac{\sin i}{\sin r}$
<marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Identify the variables
steps.content: We need to state the relationship between the angle of incidence ($i$) and the angle of refraction ($r$) for light entering a transparent block from air. This topic is covered in **Topic C.3 Wave phenomena**.

steps.title: State the equation
steps.content:
Using the **Refractive Index Definition** approach, the equation is defined as the ratio of the sine of the angle of incidence to the sine of the angle of refraction:

$$n = \frac{\sin i}{\sin r}$$

steps.title: Reference data booklet
steps.content:
This equation is a specific case of **Snell's Law** provided in the data booklet. When light travels from air ($n_1 \approx 1$) to a medium ($n_2 = n$), the general formula simplifies:

$$n_{1}\sin\theta_{1}=n_{2}\sin\theta_{2} \implies 1 \cdot \sin i = n \cdot \sin r \implies n = \frac{\sin i}{\sin r}$$

steps.title: State General Snell's Law
steps.content:
First, we look at the **Waves** section of the data booklet to find the general equation for refraction (Snell's Law), which relates the refractive indices and angles of two media:

$$n_1 \sin \theta_1 = n_2 \sin \theta_2$$

Here, $n_1$ and $n_2$ are the refractive indices, and $\theta_1$ and $\theta_2$ are the angles made with the normal.

steps.title: Define variables for air and block
steps.content:
We identify the specific values for our situation where light enters a block from air:

*   **Medium 1 (Air):** The refractive index of air is approximately $1$, so we substitute $n_1 \approx 1$. The angle of incidence is $\theta_1 = i$.
*   **Medium 2 (Block):** The refractive index is $n_2 = n$. The angle of refraction is $\theta_2 = r$.

highlights.text: enters a transparent block from air

steps.title: Substitute and rearrange
steps.content:
Now we substitute these values into the general equation:

$$1 \cdot \sin i = n \cdot \sin r$$

Rearranging to express the relationship for the refractive index $n$, we get:

$$n = \frac{\sin i}{\sin r}$$

parts.content: Calculate the angle of refraction in glass of refractive index $n = 1.50$.
parts.markscheme:
- $\sin r = \dfrac{\sin 30^\circ}{1.50} = 0.333 \Rightarrow r = 19.5^\circ$
- Correct substitution
<marks>1 mark</marks> 
- Correct final answer <marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Identify the relevant formula
steps.content:
To solve this problem, we use **Snell's Law**, which describes how light bends when moving from one medium to another. This is covered in **Topic C.3: Wave phenomena**.

From the data booklet, the formula is:

$$n_{1}\sin\theta_{1}=n_{2}\sin\theta_{2}$$

Where:
- $n_1$ is the refractive index of the first medium (air)
- $\theta_1$ is the angle of incidence
- $n_2$ is the refractive index of the second medium (glass)
- $\theta_2$ is the angle of refraction

steps.title: Determine the known values
steps.content:
From the question, we identify the following values:
- Refractive index of air, $n_1 \approx 1.00$
- Angle of incidence, $\theta_1 = 30^\circ$
- Refractive index of glass, $n_2 = 1.50$

steps.title: Substitute and solve for sin(θ₂)
steps.content:
Substitute the values into Snell's Law and solve for $\sin \theta_2$:

$$\begin{aligned} 1.00 \cdot \sin(30^\circ) &= 1.50 \cdot \sin(\theta_2) \\ \sin(\theta_2) &= \frac{1.00 \cdot \sin(30^\circ)}{1.50} \\ \sin(\theta_2) &= \frac{0.50}{1.50} \\ \sin(\theta_2) &= 0.333... \end{aligned}$$

This substitution step is typically worth **1 mark** in the IB markscheme.

steps.title: Calculate the refraction angle
steps.content:
To find the angle $\theta_2$, we take the inverse sine (arcsin) of the result. 

$$\theta_2 = \arcsin(0.333...) \approx 19.5^\circ$$

Providing the final answer rounded correctly to 3 significant figures earns the second mark.

gdc.instructions:
1. Set angle mode to Degrees.
2. Use the arcsin function: asin(sin(30) / 1.50).

ti84.instructions:
1. Ensure the calculator is in DEGREE mode: Press [MODE], highlight 'DEGREE', and press [ENTER].
2. Calculate the sine: Press [SIN] [30] [ENTER].
3. Divide by the refractive index: Press [/] [1.50] [ENTER].
4. Find the angle: Press [2nd] [SIN] [2nd] [(-)] (to use 'ANS') [ENTER].

desmos.instructions:
1. Click the 'Settings' (wrench icon) and ensure 'Degrees' is selected.
2. Type: arcsin(sin(30) / 1.50)

calculator.description: Calculate the angle of refraction using the inverse sine function.

questions.specification: A student wants to determine the speed of electromagnetic waves inside a microwave oven. They remove the rotating plate and place a flat slab of chocolate on the base of the oven. After heating for a few seconds, the chocolate begins to melt at fixed points. The frequency of the microwave is printed on the back of the oven: $f = 2.45\,\mathrm{GHz}$.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic C - Wave behaviour > C.4 Standing waves and resonance > C.4.1 Formation and properties of standing waves
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.4.1 Formation and properties of standing waves
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Core of the experiment is formation of standing waves in the microwave cavity, producing nodes/antinodes and fixed heating points (melted spots).

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic C - Wave behaviour > C.4 Standing waves and resonance > C.4.2 Standing wave patterns in systems
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.4.2 Standing wave patterns in systems
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Uses the spatial pattern of a standing wave (spacing between hot spots related to λ/2) to infer wavelength and hence wave speed; also addresses effect of rotation on the pattern.

parts.content: Explain how standing waves are formed inside the microwave oven and why they result in specific points of heating.
parts.markscheme:
- Standing waves form due to reflection of microwaves off the metal walls of the oven <marks>A1</marks>
- Antinodes correspond to points of maximum electric field where localized heating occurs <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Formation of standing waves
steps.content:
In a microwave oven, the magnetron generates microwaves that travel towards the metal walls. These waves undergo **reflection** when they hit the walls. 

When the incident waves and the reflected waves (which have the same frequency and similar amplitude) travel in opposite directions through the oven, they undergo **superposition**. This interference creates a permanent pattern known as a standing wave.

highlights.text: reflection of microwaves off the metal walls of the oven

steps.title: Localized heating at antinodes
steps.content:
The standing wave pattern consists of **nodes** (points of zero displacement) and **antinodes** (points of maximum displacement). 

At the **antinodes**, the electric field intensity is at its maximum. Since the energy transferred to the chocolate (heating) is proportional to the square of the electric field's amplitude, these specific points reach higher temperatures. This explains why the chocolate melts at fixed, localized points rather than evenly across the slab.

highlights.text: Antinodes correspond to points of maximum electric field where localized heating occurs

steps.title: Reviewing relevant concepts
steps.content: This phenomenon is a core part of **Topic C.4: Standing waves and resonance**. To earn full marks in an IB exam, you must explicitly mention the **reflection** from the walls and link the **antinodes** to the maximum intensity or heating.

parts.content: Describe how the student could use the melted chocolate to determine the wavelength of the microwave radiation.
parts.markscheme:
- Measure the distance between consecutive melted spots on the chocolate <marks>M1</marks>
- Recognize this distance is half the wavelength; thus $\lambda = 2 \times \text{spacing}$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify the wave pattern
steps.content: When the rotating plate is removed, the microwaves reflect off the oven walls and interfere with each other. This creates a **standing wave** pattern. The "fixed points" where the chocolate melts are the **antinodes**, which are the positions of maximum energy where the heating is most intense.

steps.title: Measure adjacent spots
steps.content:
To determine the wavelength, the student should first use a ruler to measure the distance between two **consecutive** (adjacent) melted spots on the chocolate. 

This measurement represents the distance between two neighboring antinodes in the standing wave pattern.

highlights.text: Measure the distance between consecutive melted spots on the chocolate

steps.title: Relate distance to wavelength
steps.content:
In a standing wave, the distance between two adjacent antinodes is exactly **half a wavelength** ($\frac{\lambda}{2}$). 

To find the full wavelength ($\lambda$), the student simply needs to multiply the measured distance by two:

$$\lambda = 2 \times \text{distance between spots}$$

highlights.text: Recognize this distance is half the wavelength; thus $\lambda = 2 \times \text{spacing}$

steps.title: Identify the standing wave
steps.content: In a microwave oven, the waves reflect off the walls and interfere to form a **standing wave**. The spots where the chocolate melts are the antinodes (points of maximum intensity). This concept is covered in **Topic C.4: Standing waves and resonance**.

highlights.text: chocolate begins to melt at fixed points

steps.title: Measure across multiple intervals
steps.content: To get a more precise measurement, use a ruler to measure the total distance across several melted spots (for example, from the 1st spot to the 5th spot). This is more accurate than measuring just one single gap because it reduces the impact of measurement error.

steps.title: Calculate the average spacing
steps.content: Divide the total measured distance by the number of gaps between the spots. For example, if you measured across 5 spots, there are 4 gaps. This result gives the average distance between consecutive melted spots.

steps.title: Relate spacing to wavelength
steps.content:
In a standing wave, the distance between consecutive antinodes (melted spots) is equal to half a wavelength ($ \frac{\lambda}{2} $). 

Multiply your average spacing by 2 to determine the wavelength, $\lambda$.

parts.content: The student observes that the distance between melted regions is $d = 6.0\,\mathrm{cm}$. Calculate the speed of the microwave radiation in air.
parts.markscheme:
- Wavelength is $\lambda = 2 \times 0.060 = 0.12\,\mathrm{m}$ <marks>A1</marks>
- Use $v = f \lambda = 2.45 \times 10^9 \times 0.12 = 2.94 \times 10^8\,\mathrm{m\,s^{-1}}$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Analyze the standing wave
steps.content:
In a microwave oven, the waves reflect off the walls to form a **standing wave pattern**. The chocolate melts at the **antinodes**, which are the positions of maximum amplitude (and thus maximum energy). 

From **Topic C.4 Standing waves**, we know that the distance between two consecutive antinodes is equal to half a wavelength ($\frac{\lambda}{2}$).

highlights.text: distance between melted regions is d = 6.0 cm

steps.title: Calculate the wavelength
steps.content:
Since the distance $d = 6.0\text{ cm}$ represents half a wavelength, we can find the full wavelength ($\lambda$) and convert it to meters:

$$\begin{aligned} \frac{\lambda}{2} &= 6.0\text{ cm} \\ \lambda &= 2 \times 6.0\text{ cm} \\ \lambda &= 12.0\text{ cm} = 0.12\text{ m} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate 2 times 0.06.

ti84.instructions: Multiply 2 by 0.06.

desmos.instructions: Evaluate 2 * 0.06.

calculator.description: Calculate the wavelength in meters.

steps.title: Convert frequency to Hertz
steps.content:
The frequency is given in gigahertz ($1\text{ GHz} = 10^9\text{ Hz}$). We must convert this to standard units for our calculation:

$$f = 2.45\text{ GHz} = 2.45 \times 10^9\text{ Hz}$$

steps.title: Calculate the wave speed
steps.content:
Now, we use the wave equation from the **Waves** section of the data booklet to find the speed ($v$):

$$\begin{aligned} v &= f\lambda \\ v &= (2.45 \times 10^9\text{ Hz}) \times (0.12\text{ m}) \\ v &= 2.94 \times 10^8\text{ m s}^{-1} \end{aligned}$$

This value is very close to the accepted speed of light ($c \approx 3.00 \times 10^8\text{ m s}^{-1}$), which confirms our result is realistic.

gdc.instructions: Multiply 2.45E9 by 0.12.

ti84.instructions: Multiply 2.45E9 by 0.12.

calculator.description: Calculate the wave speed by multiplying frequency and wavelength.

parts.content: State one assumption made in this experiment.
parts.markscheme:
- Microwaves form perfect standing waves OR that wave speed in air $\approx$ in vacuum <marks>B1</marks>

1 mark total

steps.title: Identify the experimental basis
steps.content: In this experiment, we observe that the chocolate melts at **fixed points**. To calculate the speed of the microwaves using the frequency and the distance between these points, we rely on the specific physics of wave interference. This is covered in **Topic C.4: Standing waves and resonance**.

steps.title: Assumption of standing waves
steps.content:
The primary assumption of the **Standing Wave Formation** approach is that the microwaves reflecting off the internal walls of the oven interfere with the incoming waves to form a perfect, stationary pattern. 

We assume that the melt spots occur at the **antinodes** (points of maximum amplitude/intensity) of this standing wave. For the distance between these spots to accurately represent half a wavelength ($\frac{\lambda}{2}$), the pattern must be stable and perfectly formed.

steps.title: Assumption of wave speed
steps.content: Another valid assumption relates to the medium. The experiment is conducted in the air inside the microwave, but calculations often use the speed of light in a vacuum ($c = 3.00 \times 10^{8} \text{ m s}^{-1}$). Therefore, we assume that the **wave speed in air is approximately equal to the speed in a vacuum**.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
To earn the mark, you only need to state one of these clearly. 

**Possible answers:**
1. Microwaves form perfect standing waves inside the oven.
2. The wave speed in air is approximately the same as the wave speed in a vacuum.

steps.title: Identify the medium
steps.content: In this experiment, electromagnetic waves (microwaves) are traveling through the air inside the oven cavity to reach the chocolate. This falls under **Topic C.2 (Wave model)**, which describes how waves propagate through different media.

steps.title: Apply wave speed constants
steps.content:
When performing calculations for electromagnetic waves, we typically refer to the speed of light constant $c$ found in the **Physical Constants** section of the data booklet:

$$c = 3.00 \times 10^{8} \text{ m s}^{-1}$$

This value specifically represents the speed of light in a vacuum.

steps.title: State the assumption
steps.content:
Because the microwaves are actually traveling through air rather than a vacuum, we must make a simplifying assumption to use the standard value of $c$. 

The assumption is that the **wave speed in air is approximately equal to the wave speed in a vacuum**. 

In reality, electromagnetic waves slow down slightly in air, but for IB Physics experiments of this type, the difference is negligible enough to ignore.

parts.content: Explain why this experiment does not work with the rotating plate.
parts.markscheme:
- The rotating plate moves the chocolate through antinodes and nodes, averaging heat distribution <marks>A1</marks>
- This prevents fixed melt spots from forming, so the standing wave cannot be measured <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Movement through the wave
steps.content: Inside a microwave oven, a stationary standing wave pattern is established. This pattern consists of **antinodes** (points of maximum intensity/heating) and **nodes** (points of minimum intensity). When the plate rotates, it carries the chocolate slab through these high-energy and low-energy regions continuously.

highlights.text: Explain why this experiment does not work with the rotating plate.

steps.title: Thermal averaging of energy
steps.content: Because any single point on the chocolate is moving, it doesn't stay at an antinode long enough to melt in isolation. Instead, it moves through a series of nodes and antinodes, receiving a **time-averaged** amount of energy. This results in the thermal energy being distributed much more uniformly across the surface of the chocolate.

steps.title: Disappearance of melt spots
steps.content: For this experiment to work, we rely on the formation of fixed, distinct melt spots to measure the distance between antinodes (which is $\frac{\lambda}{2}$). Since the rotating plate averages out the heat distribution, these fixed spots never form, making it impossible to measure the wavelength $\lambda$ and calculate the wave speed using $$v = f\lambda$$.

steps.title: Identify the measurement requirement
steps.content: To determine the speed of the waves using $v = f\lambda$, we first need to find the wavelength $\lambda$. This experiment relies on **standing waves**, where the distance between two adjacent antinodes (points of maximum intensity) is exactly $\frac{\lambda}{2}$. For this to be measurable, the chocolate must stay in a fixed position relative to the wave pattern to develop distinct melt spots.

steps.title: Explain the effect of rotation
steps.content: When the plate rotates, any single point on the chocolate slab is constantly moving through different regions of the standing wave. It passes through multiple **antinodes** (high energy) and **nodes** (low energy). This motion causes the thermal energy to be distributed across the surface rather than concentrated at specific points.

highlights.text: The rotating plate moves the chocolate through antinodes and nodes, averaging heat distribution

steps.title: Conclude on spatial localization
steps.content: Because the heat is averaged out, the chocolate heats up more uniformly. This prevents the formation of **fixed, discrete melt spots** at the antinode positions. Without these spatially localized spots, the student cannot measure the physical distance $\frac{\lambda}{2}$, making it impossible to calculate the wavelength or the speed of the wave.

highlights.text: This prevents fixed melt spots from forming, so the standing wave cannot be measured

parts.content: In a second attempt, the student forgets to remove the rotating platform. After running the microwave, the entire chocolate slab melts uniformly. Explain why this occurred and why it prevents determining the speed of microwaves.
parts.markscheme:
- The rotation spreads the heating evenly across the chocolate <marks>A1</marks>
- Uniform melting hides antinodes; no spatial variation means no wavelength data can be extracted <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Analyze energy distribution
steps.content:
Inside a microwave, waves reflect off the walls and interfere to create a **standing wave pattern**. This pattern consists of points of maximum energy called **antinodes** (where the chocolate melts) and points of minimum energy called **nodes**.

This concept is a key part of **Topic C.4: Standing waves**, where we learn that standing waves have a fixed spatial distribution of energy.

steps.title: Effect of the rotation
steps.content:
When the platform rotates, the chocolate slab moves through the different regions of the standing wave pattern. Every part of the chocolate eventually passes through multiple antinodes and nodes. 

This movement **averages out the energy** delivered to the slab, spreading the thermal energy evenly across the surface. This is why the slab melts uniformly instead of at specific points.

highlights.text: The rotation spreads the heating evenly across the chocolate

steps.title: Why measurement is impossible
steps.content:
To determine the speed of the wave ($v$), we use the wave equation from the data booklet:

$$v = f\lambda$$

In the first experiment, the student finds the wavelength $\lambda$ by measuring the distance between distinct melt spots (the distance between two antinodes is $\frac{\lambda}{2}$). 

Because the rotation causes **uniform melting**, there are no longer distinct antinodes visible. Without these spatial markers, the student cannot measure the wavelength, and therefore cannot calculate the speed.

highlights.text: Uniform melting hides antinodes; no spatial variation means no wavelength data can be extracted
