labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Fixed-ends string harmonics, node/antinode positions and how patterns relate to harmonic number are standing-wave patterns in systems.

parts.content: Describe how the positions of nodes and antinodes are identified in the stroboscopic view.
parts.markscheme:
- Nodes appear stationary under strobe light
<marks>1 mark</marks>
- Antinodes oscillate with maximum amplitude
<marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: State what happens to the wave speed on the string when the tension is increased.
parts.markscheme:
Wave speed increases with increased tension
<marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Identify the relevant property
steps.content: To answer this, we need to recall how the properties of a medium—specifically a stretched string—affect the speed of a wave traveling through it. This is a key concept in **Topic C: Wave behaviour**.

steps.title: State the relationship
steps.content:
Using the **Direct Relationship Property**, we know that for a wave on a string, the wave speed ($v$) is directly proportional to the square root of the tension ($T$) in the string:

$$v \propto \sqrt{T}$$

This means that as the tension increases, the wave speed must also increase.

steps.title: Final Conclusion
steps.content:
Since the speed is proportional to the square root of the tension, increasing the tension results in a higher wave speed.

**Final Answer:**
The wave speed increases.

steps.title: Analyze tension and frequency
steps.content: To understand how wave speed changes, let's first look at the relationship between tension and frequency. From common experience with musical instruments, we know that tightening a string (increasing the tension) results in a higher pitch. In physics terms, a higher pitch means that the frequency $f$ of the standing wave increases.

steps.title: Determine constant wavelength
steps.content:
For a standing wave on a string that is fixed at both ends, the possible wavelengths are determined by the length of the string $L$. The formula for the wavelength of the $n^{th}$ harmonic is:

$$\lambda = \frac{2L}{n}$$

Since the length of the string and the harmonic number (the pattern we are observing) remain constant, the wavelength $\lambda$ does not change when the tension is adjusted.

steps.title: Apply the wave equation
steps.content:
We can now relate these variables using the wave equation found in the **Waves** section of your data booklet:

$$v = f\lambda$$

Because the frequency $f$ increases and the wavelength $\lambda$ remains constant, the product $f\lambda$ must increase. Therefore, the wave speed $v$ increases.

steps.title: State final conclusion
steps.content: As a result of the increased tension leading to a higher frequency for the same wavelength, the wave speed on the string increases.

steps.title: Identify the trend
steps.content:
When the tension in the string is increased, the **wave speed increases**.

To understand why, we can look at the mechanical forces acting on the individual segments of the string as the wave passes through them.

steps.title: Tension as restoring force
steps.content:
A wave propagates through a string because displaced segments are pulled back toward the equilibrium position by the tension from the rest of the string. This tension acts as the **restoring force** for the oscillation. 

If we increase the tension, we are effectively increasing the magnitude of the restoring force acting on every segment of the string. This concept relates to **Topic A.2 (Forces and momentum)** where we study how forces cause motion.

steps.title: Apply Newton's second law
steps.content:
We can use Newton's second law from the data booklet to see how this increased force affects the motion of the string segments:

$$F = ma$$

Since the mass ($m$) of a string segment is constant, an increase in the restoring force ($F$) resulting from the higher tension leads to a **greater acceleration** ($a$) of the string segments.

steps.title: Increased propagation speed
steps.content:
Because the string segments have a higher acceleration, they move to their displaced positions and back to equilibrium much faster. Since the wave pulse is essentially the "transfer" of this motion from one segment to the next, the disturbance travels along the string more quickly.

Therefore, the wave speed on the string increases.

parts.content: Explain, using the principle of superposition, how a standing wave is formed on the string.
parts.markscheme:
- Standing wave results from superposition of two identical waves moving in opposite directions
<marks>1 mark</marks>
- Interference produces a pattern of nodes and antinodes that appears stationary
<marks>1 mark</marks>

methods.label: Superposition of Incident and Reflected Waves

steps.title: Wave Reflection
steps.content:
When a wave (the **incident wave**) travels along the string and hits the fixed end, it undergoes reflection. Because the end is fixed, the wave reflects back in the opposite direction. 

This reflected wave is essentially identical to the incident wave—it has the same frequency, wavelength, and amplitude—but it is traveling in the opposite direction toward the source.

highlights.text: Explain, using the principle of superposition, how a standing wave is formed on the string.

steps.title: Principle of Superposition
steps.content:
As the incident and reflected waves pass through each other, they **superpose**. 

The principle of superposition states that the resultant displacement of the string at any point is the vector sum of the displacements of the individual waves. In this case, we have two identical waves moving in opposite directions.

$$\text{Total Displacement} = y_{\text{incident}} + y_{\text{reflected}}$$

highlights.text: Standing wave results from superposition of two identical waves moving in opposite directions

steps.title: Formation of Standing Pattern
steps.content:
This continuous superposition creates a stable interference pattern:

*   **Nodes:** Points where the two waves always interfere destructively (cancel each other out), resulting in zero displacement.
*   **Antinodes:** Points where the waves interfere constructively, resulting in maximum displacement.

Because the positions of these nodes and antinodes do not move along the string, the wave is described as "standing" or stationary.

highlights.text: Interference produces a pattern of nodes and antinodes that appears stationary

parts.content: Determine the effect of increasing the wave speed on the frequency of the standing wave if the harmonic number remains constant.
parts.markscheme:
- Wave speed $v = f\lambda$, and for fixed $n$, $\lambda$ is constant
<marks>1 mark</marks>
- So $f$ increases when wave speed increases
<marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Analyze the standing wave
steps.content:
For a standing wave on a string fixed at both ends, the wavelength $\lambda$ is determined by the length of the string $L$ and the harmonic number $n$. 

The relationship is given by:
$$\lambda = \frac{2L}{n}$$

Since the question states that the string remains fixed (constant $L$) and the **harmonic number remains constant**, the wavelength $\lambda$ must also be constant.

steps.title: Apply the wave equation
steps.content:
Now we look at the fundamental wave equation from the **Waves** section of your data booklet:

$$v = f\lambda$$

In this equation, $v$ is the wave speed and $f$ is the frequency. This equation shows the direct relationship between speed, frequency, and wavelength.

steps.title: Determine the frequency effect
steps.content:
To see how the frequency changes, we can rearrange the wave equation to solve for $f$:

$$f = \frac{v}{\lambda}$$

Since we established in Step 0 that $\lambda$ is constant, we can see that $f$ is directly proportional to $v$. Therefore, if the wave speed $v$ increases, the frequency $f$ must also increase to maintain the same wavelength pattern (harmonic).

steps.title: Final Conclusion
steps.content:
In an IB exam, you would earn marks by stating that the wavelength remains constant for a fixed harmonic and then concluding that frequency increases with wave speed.

**Answer:** The frequency of the standing wave increases.

steps.title: Identify the standing wave formula
steps.content:
For a string fixed at both ends, the possible wavelengths $\lambda$ for a standing wave depend on the length of the string $L$ and the harmonic number $n$. This relationship is given by:

$$\lambda = \frac{2L}{n}$$

From the **Waves** section of your data booklet, we also know the wave equation:

$$v = f\lambda$$

steps.title: Derive frequency in terms of speed
steps.content:
To see how the frequency $f$ changes, let's rearrange the wave equation and substitute the expression for wavelength:

$$\begin{aligned} f &= \frac{v}{\lambda} \\ f &= \frac{v}{\left(\frac{2L}{n}\right)} \\ f &= \frac{nv}{2L} \end{aligned}$$

This is the general formula for the frequency of any harmonic on a string fixed at both ends.

steps.title: Identify constant variables
steps.content:
The question specifies that the harmonic number remains constant, and we assume the length of the string $L$ also stays the same. 

In our derived equation $$f = \left( \frac{n}{2L} \right) v$$, the term in the parentheses is a constant. This means that frequency is directly proportional to wave speed:

$$f \propto v$$

steps.title: Determine the final effect
steps.content:
Because frequency and wave speed are directly proportional, increasing the wave speed will result in an **increase** in the frequency of the standing wave.

In an IB exam, you earn marks for:
1.  Recognizing that wavelength $\lambda$ remains constant if $n$ and $L$ are fixed (1 mark).
2.  Stating that the frequency $f$ increases (1 mark).

parts.content:
A student incorrectly claims that antinodes are points of destructive interference.
Comment on this statement and provide the correct explanation.
parts.markscheme:
- Statement is incorrect: antinodes are formed by constructive interference
<marks>1 mark</marks>
- Destructive interference occurs at nodes, where the displacements cancel
<marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Evaluate the student's claim
steps.content:
The student's claim is **incorrect**. In the study of standing waves (which you can review in **Topic C.4**), the terms for interference points have been reversed. 

Standing waves are formed by the superposition of two waves of the same frequency and amplitude traveling in opposite directions. Depending on how these waves meet, they either reinforce or cancel each other out.

highlights.text: antinodes are points of destructive interference

steps.title: Correct the antinode definition
steps.content:
Antinodes are actually the points of **maximum displacement** (maximum amplitude). This occurs because the two traveling waves meet in phase and undergo **constructive interference**.

At these points, the displacements of the individual waves add together to create the largest possible oscillation.

steps.title: Correct the node definition
steps.content:
The description the student used actually applies to **nodes**. Nodes are the points where the string does not move at all (zero displacement). 

This happens because the two waves meet out of phase, leading to **destructive interference**, where their individual displacements always cancel each other out.

steps.title: Final Summary for Marks
steps.content:
To secure both marks on an IB exam, your explanation should clearly state:
1. The student is incorrect: antinodes are formed by **constructive interference**.
2. **Destructive interference** occurs at the nodes, where the displacements of the waves cancel out.

steps.title: Address the student's error
steps.content: The student's claim is incorrect. In the study of **Topic C.4 (Standing waves and resonance)**, we learn that standing waves are formed by the **superposition** of two waves of the same frequency and amplitude traveling in opposite directions. The student has confused the types of interference occurring at specific points.

highlights.text: antinodes are points of destructive interference

steps.title: Explain antinodes and constructive interference
steps.content:
Antinodes are the points in a standing wave where the string oscillates with the **maximum amplitude**. This happens because the two counter-propagating waves meet in phase, leading to **constructive interference**.

According to the principle of superposition, the resultant displacement $y$ is the sum of the individual displacements:

$$y_{total} = y_1 + y_2$$

At an antinode, these displacements reinforce each other to create the largest possible vibration.

steps.title: Explain nodes and destructive interference
steps.content:
**Destructive interference** actually occurs at the **nodes**. These are positions along the string where the two waves are always $180^{\circ}$ (or $\pi$ radians) out of phase. 

Because the waves have equal amplitude but opposite displacements at these points, they completely cancel each other out, resulting in zero net displacement at all times.

steps.title: Summary for exam success
steps.content:
To secure both marks in an IB exam, your explanation must include:
1. A clear statement that the student is wrong because antinodes are formed by **constructive interference**.
2. An explanation that **destructive interference** occurs at the nodes, where displacements cancel out to zero.

questions.specification:
A student investigates how the refractive index of a transparent material varies with the wavelength of light in the visible spectrum. The collected data are well described by the relationship: $n(\lambda) = A + \frac{B}{\lambda^2}$, where $\lambda$ is the wavelength in nanometres (nm), and $A$, $B$ are constants to be determined. The student uses a set of monochromatic light sources and glass prism to measure the refractive index of light in glass at different wavelengths. The obtained results are shown below.

| Wavelength $\lambda$ (nm) | Refractive Index ($n$) | $1/\lambda^2$ (nm$^{-2}$) |
|---|---|---|
| 450 | 1.558 | |
| 480 | 1.546 | |
| 510 | 1.538 | |
| 540 | 1.532 | |
| 570 | 1.528 | |
| 600 | 1.525 | |

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic C - Wave behaviour > C.3 Wave phenomena > C.3.1 Reflection, refraction, and diffraction
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.3.1 Reflection, refraction, and diffraction
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question focuses on the experimental measurement and modeling of the refractive index of glass using a prism and monochromatic light sources. This falls directly under Topic C.3.1, which covers refraction and its properties.

parts.content: Determine the units of the constants A and B.
parts.markscheme:
- State that A is dimensionless: As the refractive index $n$ has no units <marks>A1</marks>

- State that B has units of $\text{nm}^2$: Since it is added to $A$ after dividing by $\lambda^2$ (or because $B/\lambda^2$ must be dimensionless) <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Analyze the Refractive Index
steps.content:
First, we need to recall the nature of the refractive index, $n$. In physics, the refractive index is defined as a ratio of speeds (the speed of light in a vacuum divided by the speed of light in the material). 

Because it is a ratio of two quantities with the same units (m/s), the refractive index $n$ is **dimensionless**, meaning it has no units. This is a crucial starting point for using dimensional homogeneity.

steps.title: Apply Dimensional Homogeneity
steps.content:
The principle of **dimensional homogeneity** states that for an equation to be physically valid, every term must have the same units. The given relationship is:

$$n = A + \frac{B}{\lambda^2}$$

Since the left side ($n$) has no units, every individual term on the right side must also have no units.

steps.title: Determine Units of A
steps.content:
Looking at the first term on the right side, $A$ stands alone. According to our principle, $A$ must have the same units as $n$.

Since $n$ is dimensionless, **$A$ is also dimensionless** (it has no units).

steps.title: Determine Units of B
steps.content:
Now we look at the second term, $\frac{B}{\lambda^2}$. This whole term must also be dimensionless. We are told that $\lambda$ is the wavelength in nanometres (nm), so the units of $\lambda^2$ are $\text{nm}^2$.

To make the fraction dimensionless, the units in the numerator must cancel out the units in the denominator:

$$\text{units of } \frac{B}{\text{nm}^2} = \text{dimensionless}$$

Therefore, the constant **$B$ must have units of $\text{nm}^2$**.

steps.title: Final Answer Summary
steps.content:
In an IB exam, you earn marks by clearly stating the units for each constant based on the properties of the equation:

*   **$A$**: Dimensionless (No units)
*   **$B$**: $\text{nm}^2$

steps.title: Identify the units of n
steps.content:
Before we rearrange the equation, we need to know the units of the refractive index $n$. 

In the IB syllabus, specifically in **Topic C.3: Wave phenomena**, we learn that the refractive index is a ratio of speeds (or the ratio of sines of angles). Because it is a ratio of two identical quantities, it has no units.

$$n = \text{dimensionless (no units)}$$

steps.title: Analyze the constant A
steps.content:
The equation provided is:

$$n = A + \frac{B}{\lambda^2}$$

In physics, for an equation to be valid, every term added together must have the same units. Since the refractive index $n$ is dimensionless, both $A$ and the term $\frac{B}{\lambda^2}$ must also be dimensionless. 

Therefore, **$A$ is dimensionless**.

steps.title: Rearrange to isolate B
steps.content:
To find the units of the constant $B$, let's use the algebraic rearrangement approach to isolate $B$. 

First, subtract $A$ from both sides:
$$n - A = \frac{B}{\lambda^2}$$

Next, multiply both sides by $\lambda^2$:
$$B = (n - A) \cdot \lambda^2$$

steps.title: Determine units of B
steps.content:
Now we substitute the units into our rearranged equation for $B$:

1. The term $(n - A)$ is the difference between two dimensionless numbers, so it is also dimensionless.
2. The question states that $\lambda$ is the wavelength in **nanometres (nm)**. Therefore, $\lambda^2$ has units of $\text{nm}^2$.

$$\text{Units of } B = (\text{dimensionless}) \cdot \text{nm}^2$$

Thus, the units of **$B$ are $\text{nm}^2$**.

steps.title: Linearize the equation
steps.content:
To solve this using a graphical approach, we compare the given relationship to the equation of a straight line, $y = mx + c$. 

The student is investigating how the refractive index $n$ varies with the wavelength $\lambda$ using the formula:
$$n = A + \frac{B}{\lambda^2}$$

If we let $y = n$ and $x = \frac{1}{\lambda^2}$, the equation becomes:
$$n = B \left( \frac{1}{\lambda^2} \right) + A$$

In this form:
- $n$ is on the vertical axis ($y$)
- $\frac{1}{\lambda^2}$ is on the horizontal axis ($x$)
- $B$ is the gradient ($m$)
- $A$ is the vertical intercept ($c$)

steps.title: Determine units of A
steps.content:
From the graph of $n$ versus $1/\lambda^2$, $A$ represents the $y$-intercept. The $y$-intercept must have the same units as the $y$-axis variable. 

In physics, the **refractive index ($n$)** is a ratio of the speed of light in a vacuum to the speed of light in the medium. Because it is a ratio of two speeds (both in $\text{m s}^{-1}$), the units cancel out. Therefore, $n$ is dimensionless (it has no units). 

Since $A$ corresponds to the value of $n$ when $1/\lambda^2 = 0$, $A$ must also be **dimensionless**.

steps.title: Determine units of B
steps.content:
The constant $B$ represents the gradient ($m$) of the graph. The units of a gradient are calculated as:
$$\text{units of gradient} = \frac{\text{units of } y}{\text{units of } x}$$

We know:
- Units of $y$ ($n$) = none (dimensionless)
- Units of $x$ ($\frac{1}{\lambda^2}$) = $\text{nm}^{-2}$

Substituting these in:
$$\text{units of } B = \frac{1}{\text{nm}^{-2}} = \text{nm}^2$$

Thus, the units of $B$ are **$\text{nm}^2$**.

parts.content: Describe how the student could measure the refractive index for different wavelengths using a prism and a set of monochromatic light sources.
parts.markscheme:
- Direct a beam of monochromatic light at a known angle onto the glass-air interface and measure the angle of refraction <marks>M1</marks>

- Apply Snell’s law: $n = \frac{\sin \theta_i}{\sin \theta_r}$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Select a wavelength
steps.content: To investigate the relationship between wavelength and refractive index, the student must perform the experiment for each of the provided light sources. For one wavelength $(\lambda)$, use a single monochromatic source to ensure a single, clear path for the beam.

steps.title: Measure angles of incidence
steps.content:
Direct the monochromatic beam at a non-zero angle of incidence to the first face of the glass prism. Using a protractor or an optical bench setup, measure:
1. The **angle of incidence** $(\theta_i)$ between the incident ray and the normal to the prism surface.
2. The **angle of refraction** $(\theta_r)$ between the refracted ray and the normal inside the glass.

highlights.text: Direct a beam of monochromatic light at a known angle onto the glass-air interface and measure the angle of refraction

steps.title: Apply Snell's Law
steps.content:
Using the measured angles, calculate the refractive index $(n)$ of the glass. Since the light is traveling from air $(n \approx 1)$ into the glass prism, we use the formula for refraction from the data booklet:

$$n_1 \sin \theta_1 = n_2 \sin \theta_2$$

Substituting $n_1 = 1$ (air), $\theta_1 = \theta_i$, and $\theta_2 = \theta_r$, we solve for the refractive index of the glass $(n)$:

$$n = \frac{\sin \theta_i}{\sin \theta_r}$$

This calculation provides one data point for the table. Repeat this process for all other monochromatic sources.

steps.title: Set up the experiment
steps.content: To find the refractive index $n$ using the **Critical Angle Method**, we use a monochromatic light source and a glass prism. We direct a narrow beam of light through the prism so that it hits the internal glass-air boundary at the second surface.

steps.title: Determine the critical angle
steps.content: Slowly increase the angle of incidence at this internal boundary. We are looking for the specific angle, called the **critical angle** ($c$), where the light is refracted at $90^\circ$ (travelling exactly along the boundary) or just begins to undergo total internal reflection.

steps.title: Apply Snell's Law
steps.content:
From the **Waves** section of the data booklet, we use Snell’s law. At the critical angle, the light moves from glass ($n_1 = n$) to air ($n_2 \approx 1$) with an angle of refraction of $90^\circ$.

$$\begin{aligned} n_1 \sin \theta_1 &= n_2 \sin \theta_2 \\ n \sin(c) &= 1 \cdot \sin(90^\circ) \\ n &= \frac{1}{\sin(c)} \end{aligned}$$

By measuring the angle $c$, we can calculate the refractive index $n$.

steps.title: Repeat for all wavelengths
steps.content: Finally, the student must repeat this entire procedure for each monochromatic light source in the set (e.g., $450\text{ nm}$ to $600\text{ nm}$). Since the refractive index varies with wavelength, the critical angle $c$ will be slightly different for each color of light.

steps.title: Identify the relevant physics principle
steps.content:
To find the refractive index ($n$), we use **Snell's Law**, which describes the relationship between the angle of incidence ($\theta_1$) and the angle of refraction ($\theta_2$) as light moves from air into the glass prism.

From the Data Booklet:
$$n_1 \sin\theta_1 = n_2 \sin\theta_2$$

Since the light starts in air, we can assume the initial refractive index $n_1 \approx 1.00$. This simplifies the equation for the glass ($n_2 = n$) to:
$$\sin \theta_i = n \sin \theta_r$$

steps.title: Collect data for multiple angles
steps.content:
For a single monochromatic light source (one wavelength $\lambda$):
1. Direct a narrow beam of light at the prism at a specific angle of incidence $\theta_i$.
2. Use a protractor to measure the angle of incidence and the resulting angle of refraction $\theta_r$ inside the glass.
3. **Crucially**, repeat this process for several different incident angles to obtain multiple pairs of $(\theta_i, \theta_r)$. This reduces experimental error compared to taking just one measurement.

highlights.text: Direct a beam of monochromatic light at a known angle onto the glass-air interface and measure the angle of refraction

steps.title: Prepare the graphical analysis
steps.content:
To determine $n$ graphically, we transform the measurements into a linear form. By calculating the sine of each angle, we can relate them using the linear equation $y = mx$:

$$\sin \theta_i = n \cdot \sin \theta_r$$

In this model, $\sin \theta_i$ is our $y$-variable and $\sin \theta_r$ is our $x$-variable.

gdc.instructions: Set calculator to Degree mode. Use the sine function to calculate values for each measured angle.

ti84.instructions: Ensure the calculator is in Degree mode. Press [SIN] followed by the angle value, then [ENTER]. Repeat for all recorded angles.

desmos.instructions: Switch to Degree mode in settings. Type sin(angle) to get the numerical values.

calculator.description: Calculate sine values for collected angles to prepare for graphing.

steps.title: Determine refractive index from gradient
steps.content:
Plot a graph of $\sin \theta_i$ against $\sin \theta_r$. 

1. Draw a **line of best fit** through the data points.
2. Calculate the **gradient** (slope) of this line.
3. Since the gradient represents the ratio $\frac{\sin \theta_i}{\sin \theta_r}$, it is equal to the **refractive index ($n$)** for that specific wavelength.

Finally, repeat this entire process (multiple angles and graphing) for every monochromatic light source in the set to see how $n$ changes with wavelength.

gdc.instructions: Input sin(r) as X and sin(i) as Y in the statistics list. Perform a linear regression (ax+b). The coefficient 'a' is the refractive index.

ti84.instructions: Enter sin(r) values in L1 and sin(i) values in L2. Press [STAT] -> [CALC] -> [4:LinReg(ax+b)]. The value of 'a' is the refractive index.

desmos.instructions: Create a table with x1 = sin(r) and y1 = sin(i). Use the expression y1 ~ m*x1 to find the gradient 'm'.

calculator.description: Use linear regression to find the gradient (n) from the data points.

parts.content: Use the data table to complete the missing values in the third column.
parts.markscheme:
- Calculate correct values to 4 sig. figs: <marks>A1</marks>
  - $4.938 \times 10^{-6} \,\text{nm}^{-2}$
  - $4.340 \times 10^{-6} \,\text{nm}^{-2}$
  - $3.845 \times 10^{-6} \,\text{nm}^{-2}$
  - $3.429 \times 10^{-6} \,\text{nm}^{-2}$
  - $3.078 \times 10^{-6} \,\text{nm}^{-2}$
  - $2.778 \times 10^{-6} \,\text{nm}^{-2}$

1 mark total

steps.title: Understand the Calculation
steps.content:
The question asks us to fill in the third column of the table, which represents the value of $1/\lambda^2$ for each wavelength $\lambda$. 

We need to square each wavelength value from the first column and then take its reciprocal. The markscheme requires these values to be recorded to 4 significant figures.

highlights.text: Use the data table to complete the missing values in the third column.

steps.title: Calculate for first two values
steps.content:
Let's perform the calculation for the first two wavelengths using a scientific calculator.

For $\lambda = 450$ nm:
$$\frac{1}{450^2} = \frac{1}{202500} \approx 4.93827... \times 10^{-6} \text{ nm}^{-2}$$
To 4 significant figures, this is $4.938 \times 10^{-6}$.

For $\lambda = 480$ nm:
$$\frac{1}{480^2} = \frac{1}{230400} \approx 4.34027... \times 10^{-6} \text{ nm}^{-2}$$
To 4 significant figures, this is $4.340 \times 10^{-6}$.

ti84.instructions: Enter 1 / 450^2 and 1 / 480^2 on the home screen.

desmos.instructions: Enter 1/450^2 and 1/480^2 into the expression list.

calculator.description: Calculate reciprocal of squared wavelengths

steps.title: Calculate for middle values
steps.content:
Now, we calculate the values for the next two rows.

For $\lambda = 510$ nm:
$$\frac{1}{510^2} = \frac{1}{260100} \approx 3.84467... \times 10^{-6} \text{ nm}^{-2}$$
To 4 significant figures, this is $3.845 \times 10^{-6}$.

For $\lambda = 540$ nm:
$$\frac{1}{540^2} = \frac{1}{291600} \approx 3.42935... \times 10^{-6} \text{ nm}^{-2}$$
To 4 significant figures, this is $3.429 \times 10^{-6}$.

ti84.instructions: Enter 1 / 510^2 and 1 / 540^2 on the home screen.

desmos.instructions: Enter 1/510^2 and 1/540^2 into the expression list.

calculator.description: Calculate reciprocal of squared wavelengths

steps.title: Calculate for last values
steps.content:
Finally, we calculate the values for the remaining two rows.

For $\lambda = 570$ nm:
$$\frac{1}{570^2} = \frac{1}{324900} \approx 3.07787... \times 10^{-6} \text{ nm}^{-2}$$
To 4 significant figures, this is $3.078 \times 10^{-6}$.

For $\lambda = 600$ nm:
$$\frac{1}{600^2} = \frac{1}{360000} \approx 2.77777... \times 10^{-6} \text{ nm}^{-2}$$
To 4 significant figures, this is $2.778 \times 10^{-6}$.

ti84.instructions: Enter 1 / 570^2 and 1 / 600^2 on the home screen.

desmos.instructions: Enter 1/570^2 and 1/600^2 into the expression list.

calculator.description: Calculate reciprocal of squared wavelengths

steps.title: Identify the variable
steps.content:
The question asks us to calculate the value of $\frac{1}{\lambda^2}$ for each given wavelength $\lambda$ in the table. 

This is a common task in IB Physics when linearizing a relationship like $n = A + B(\frac{1}{\lambda^2})$. Understanding wave phenomena and refraction is covered in **Topic C.3: Wave phenomena**.

steps.title: Input wavelength data
steps.content:
To calculate these values efficiently, we will use the list operation feature on a Graphing Display Calculator (GDC). 

First, enter the six wavelength values into the first list (usually labeled **L1** or **List 1**).

gdc.instructions: Go to the Spreadsheet or Statistics app. Enter the wavelengths into the first column (A).

ti84.instructions: Press STAT, then select 1:Edit. Enter the wavelengths (450, 480, 510, 540, 570, 600) into L1.

desmos.instructions: Click the '+' icon and select 'Table'. Enter the wavelengths into the x1 column.

calculator.description: Enter the wavelengths into a list and perform the operation on the entire column.

steps.title: Apply list transformation
steps.content:
Now, we apply the formula transformation to the entire list to generate the reciprocal square values simultaneously. We need to calculate:

$$\text{Value} = \frac{1}{\lambda^2}$$

According to the markscheme, we must provide these values to **4 significant figures**.

gdc.instructions: In the header of the second column (B), enter the formula: = 1 / (A^2).

ti84.instructions: Arrow up to highlight the header 'L2'. Type: 1 / (L1^2) and press ENTER. The calculator will fill the column with the results.

desmos.instructions: In the header of the second column (y1), enter the formula: 1 / (x1^2).

calculator.description: Calculate the reciprocal squares for all values in the list at once.

steps.title: Final calculated values
steps.content:
After performing the calculation and rounding to 4 significant figures, the completed column is:

| $\lambda$ (nm) | $1/\lambda^2$ ($\times 10^{-6}$ nm$^{-2}$) |
| :--- | :--- |
| 450 | **4.938** |
| 480 | **4.340** |
| 510 | **3.845** |
| 540 | **3.429** |
| 570 | **3.078** |
| 600 | **2.778** |

Note that expressing these in scientific notation is standard practice for such small values (e.g., $4.938 \times 10^{-6}$ nm$^{-2}$).

parts.content: Sketch the graph of $n$ against $\frac{1}{\lambda^2}$.
parts.markscheme:
- Label axes with $x = 1/\lambda^2$ and $y = n$ with units shown <marks>A1</marks>

- Plot points accurately <marks>A1</marks>

- Draw straight line of best fit through the data points <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

steps.title: Identify the linear relationship
steps.content:
The question provides the relationship $$n = A + \frac{B}{\lambda^2}$$. To graph this linearly, we compare it to the standard equation for a straight line, $$y = mx + c$$. 

In this case:
- $y$ corresponds to $n$
- $x$ corresponds to $\frac{1}{\lambda^2}$
- $m$ (gradient) corresponds to $B$
- $c$ ($y$-intercept) corresponds to $A$

Plotting $n$ against $\frac{1}{\lambda^2}$ will yield a straight line.

steps.title: Calculate values for $1/\lambda^2$
steps.content:
Using the given wavelengths ($\lambda$), we calculate the values for our $x$-axis, $\frac{1}{\lambda^2}$. 

$$\begin{aligned} \text{For } \lambda = 450 \text{ nm: } & \frac{1}{450^2} \approx 4.94 \times 10^{-6} \text{ nm}^{-2} \\ \text{For } \lambda = 480 \text{ nm: } & \frac{1}{480^2} \approx 4.34 \times 10^{-6} \text{ nm}^{-2} \\ \text{For } \lambda = 510 \text{ nm: } & \frac{1}{510^2} \approx 3.84 \times 10^{-6} \text{ nm}^{-2} \\ \text{For } \lambda = 540 \text{ nm: } & \frac{1}{540^2} \approx 3.43 \times 10^{-6} \text{ nm}^{-2} \\ \text{For } \lambda = 570 \text{ nm: } & \frac{1}{570^2} \approx 3.08 \times 10^{-6} \text{ nm}^{-2} \\ \text{For } \lambda = 600 \text{ nm: } & \frac{1}{600^2} \approx 2.78 \times 10^{-6} \text{ nm}^{-2} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate $1/450^2$, $1/480^2$, etc. individually or using a list.

ti84.instructions: Enter 1 divided by the square of each wavelength value.

desmos.instructions: Define a list of wavelengths L = [450, 480, 510, 540, 570, 600] and then calculate 1/L^2.

calculator.description: Calculate $1/\lambda^2$ values for each wavelength in the table.

steps.title: Scale and label axes
steps.content:
To earn the first mark, label the axes clearly with units:
- **$y$-axis**: Refractive Index ($n$) — Note that $n$ has no units.
- **$x$-axis**: $1/\lambda^2$ (nm$^{-2}$)

Because the range of $n$ is small ($1.525$ to $1.558$), you should use a broken axis or start the scale from $1.50$ to ensure the data points fill the graph area.

highlights.text: Label axes with x = 1/λ² and y = n with units shown

steps.title: Plot and draw best-fit
steps.content:
Complete the graph by following these steps:
1. **Plot the points**: Mark each coordinate pair (calculated in Step 1) with a small cross ($+$ or $\times$). Accuracy is key here for the second mark.
2. **Draw the line of best fit**: Use a ruler to draw a single, thin, straight line that passes as close as possible to all points. Ensure the line shows the linear trend of $n$ increasing as $1/\lambda^2$ increases.

This follows Topic C.3 (Wave phenomena) where we analyze refraction and the relationship between speed (and thus index) and wavelength.

gdc.instructions: Enter data into spreadsheets, create a scatter plot, and perform a linear regression to see the line.

ti84.instructions:
1. Press [STAT] [EDIT] and enter $1/\lambda^2$ values into L1 and $n$ values into L2.
2. Press [2nd] [STAT PLOT], turn Plot1 ON and select Scatter.
3. Press [ZOOM] [9] to see the plot.

calculator.description: Visualize the trend and line of best fit using a scatter plot.

highlights.text: Draw straight line of best fit through the data points

parts.content: Determine the values of $A$ and $B$.
parts.markscheme:
- Determine intercept from line: $A \approx 1.48$ (Accept $1.47 - 1.49$) <marks>A1</marks>

- Determine gradient from line: $B \approx 1.52 \times 10^{4} \,\text{nm}^2$ (Accept $1.50 - 1.55 \times 10^4$) <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

parts.content: Estimate the refractive index at a wavelength of $\lambda = 700\,\text{nm}$.
parts.markscheme:
- Calculate $1/\lambda^2 \approx 2.04 \times 10^{-6} \,\text{nm}^{-2}$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Substitute into equation: $n = 1.481 + (1.52 \times 10^4)(2.04 \times 10^{-6}) \approx 1.481 + 0.031 = 1.512$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

<note>Accept answers in range $1.510 - 1.515$. Follow through from values of A and B found in Part 5.</note>

steps.title: Identify known constants
steps.content:
To estimate the refractive index at $\lambda = 700\,\text{nm}$, we need the values for constants $A$ and $B$ determined in the previous parts of the question. 

From our earlier work, we have:
- $A = 1.481$
- $B = 1.52 \times 10^4 \,\text{nm}^2$

steps.title: Calculate 1/λ²
steps.content:
The relationship provided is linear with respect to $1/\lambda^2$. First, let's calculate the value of $1/\lambda^2$ for the given wavelength:

$$\lambda = 700\,\text{nm}$$
$$\frac{1}{\lambda^2} = \frac{1}{700^2} \approx 2.04 \times 10^{-6} \,\text{nm}^{-2}$$

gdc.instructions: Enter 1 divided by 700 squared.

ti84.instructions: Type 1 / 700^2 and press ENTER.

calculator.description: Calculate the reciprocal of the square of the wavelength.

steps.title: Substitute and solve
steps.content:
Now, substitute the values of $A$, $B$, and $1/\lambda^2$ into the original equation for the refractive index:

$$n = A + B \left( \frac{1}{\lambda^2} \right)$$
$$n = 1.481 + (1.52 \times 10^4)(2.04 \times 10^{-6})$$

Calculating the second term:
$$(1.52 \times 10^4) \times (2.04 \times 10^{-6}) \approx 0.031$$

gdc.instructions: Substitute the constants into the linear equation.

calculator.description: Calculate the final refractive index.

highlights.text: Substitute into equation: $n = 1.481 + (1.52 	imes 10^4)(2.04 	imes 10^{-6}) 	imes 1.481 + 0.031 = 1.512$

steps.title: Final answer
steps.content:
Adding the values together gives the estimated refractive index:

$$n = 1.481 + 0.031 = 1.512$$

This value falls within the expected range of $1.510$ to $1.515$.

steps.title: Calculate the horizontal axis value
steps.content:
To use our linear model, we first need to determine the value of the independent variable, $\frac{1}{\lambda^2}$, for the new wavelength of $700\text{ nm}$.

$$\begin{aligned} \frac{1}{\lambda^2} &= \frac{1}{700^2} \\ &= \frac{1}{490,000} \\ &\approx 2.04 \times 10^{-6} \text{ nm}^{-2} \end{aligned}$$

This value represents the position on the horizontal axis of our graph where we want to find the refractive index.

gdc.instructions: Enter 1 divided by 700 squared.

ti84.instructions: Type 1 / 700^2 and press ENTER.

desmos.instructions: Type 1 / 700^2 into the expression bar.

calculator.description: Calculate the reciprocal square of the wavelength.

steps.title: Apply the linear model
steps.content:
The relationship given is $n = A + \frac{B}{\lambda^2}$. This corresponds to a straight-line graph $y = c + mx$, where $n$ is the $y$-value and $\frac{1}{\lambda^2}$ is the $x$-value. 

From the previous parts of the investigation, the constants were determined to be $A = 1.481$ and $B = 1.52 \times 10^4 \text{ nm}^2$. We now substitute our calculated $x$-value into this equation:

$$\begin{aligned} n &= 1.481 + (1.52 \times 10^4) \times (2.04 \times 10^{-6}) \\ n &= 1.481 + 0.031 \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Enter the expression using scientific notation.

ti84.instructions: Type 1.481 + 1.52E4 * 2.04E-6 and press ENTER.

desmos.instructions: Enter the linear equation with the substituted value.

calculator.description: Calculate the refractive index using the line of best fit equation.

steps.title: State the final estimate
steps.content:
By summing the values, we find the estimated refractive index at $700\text{ nm}$:

$$n \approx 1.512$$

This process is called **graphical extrapolation** because we are using the trend (the line of best fit) to predict a value outside the original experimental range ($450\text{ nm}$ to $600\text{ nm}$). 

In an exam, you earn one mark for correctly calculating $\frac{1}{\lambda^2}$ and a second mark for substituting it correctly into your linear model to find $n$.

parts.content: Suggest one reason why the proposed relationship $n(\lambda)$ may not give accurate values for the refractive index in infrared and ultraviolet regions of the spectrum.
parts.markscheme:
- State that the equation is empirical and accurate only within the range of data used (the visible range); it may not account for behaviour in infrared or ultraviolet regions <marks>R1</marks>

1 mark total

steps.title: Identify the experimental range
steps.content: To understand why the model might fail, we first need to look at the data the student actually collected. The wavelengths used in the experiment range from $450\text{ nm}$ to $600\text{ nm}$, which corresponds to the visible light spectrum.

steps.title: Consider the extrapolation risk
steps.content: The proposed relationship $$n(\lambda) = A + \frac{B}{\lambda^2}$$ is an empirical model, meaning it was created to fit the specific data points measured. Using this equation to predict values for infrared or ultraviolet light requires **extrapolation**—applying the model to regions far outside the experimental range.

steps.title: State the final reason
steps.content: In physics, models often lose accuracy when extrapolated because the underlying physical behavior might change (for example, glass may absorb light differently in the UV or IR ranges). Therefore, because the model was derived solely from visible light data, it may not remain valid for the significantly different wavelengths found in the infrared and ultraviolet regions.

steps.title: Analyze the experimental range
steps.content: The student investigated the refractive index within the **visible spectrum** (from 450 nm to 600 nm). The relationship $$n(\lambda) = A + \frac{B}{\lambda^2}$$ is an **empirical formula**, meaning it is a simplified model based strictly on observations within that specific range.

steps.title: Consider physical property changes
steps.content:
In the ultraviolet (UV) and infrared (IR) regions, the physical interaction between light and the material changes significantly. 

Materials often exhibit **resonance** or **absorption bands** at these wavelengths. For example:
*   In the **UV region**, photons may have enough energy to cause electronic transitions (absorption).
*   In the **IR region**, the frequency of light may match the natural vibrational frequencies of the atoms in the material (resonance).

steps.title: Evaluate formula limitations
steps.content:
The simple relationship $$n(\lambda) = A + \frac{B}{\lambda^2}$$ assumes that the refractive index changes smoothly and predictably. 

However, near resonance or absorption frequencies, the refractive index can change abruptly or behave non-linearly. Since the empirical formula does not account for these specific **physical phenomena**, it will likely fail to give accurate results outside the visible range.

steps.title: Identify the model type
steps.content:
The relationship $n(\lambda) = A + \frac{B}{\lambda^2}$ is what we call an **empirical equation**. This means it is a mathematical model designed to fit a specific set of observed data, rather than being derived from a fundamental, universal law of physics that applies everywhere.

In science, empirical formulas are great for describing behavior within a specific range, but they aren't guaranteed to work outside of it.

steps.title: Analyze the experimental range
steps.content:
Look at the data the student collected. The wavelengths range from $450\text{ nm}$ to $600\text{ nm}$, which sits entirely within the **visible spectrum**. 

The constants $A$ and $B$ are calculated specifically to make the curve match these visible light data points as closely as possible.

steps.title: Explain the limitation
steps.content:
Because the formula is an approximation based on the visible range, we cannot assume it remains accurate when we **extrapolate** (predict values outside our data set) into the **infrared** or **ultraviolet** regions. 

In these regions, the physical behavior of the glass might change—for example, the material might start absorbing light—which this simple empirical model doesn't account for. Therefore, the constants $A$ and $B$ found for visible light may no longer be valid.

steps.title: Final Conclusion
steps.content:
To get the mark, you simply need to state that the relationship is only an approximation based on the data used.

**Final Answer:** The equation is an empirical fit that is only accurate within the experimental range (visible light); it may not account for different physical behaviors in the infrared or ultraviolet regions.

| Wavelength $\lambda(\mathrm{nm})$ | Fringe spacing $s(\mathrm{~mm})$ |

| 500 | 2.50 |
| 550 | 2.75 |
| 600 | 3.00 |
| 650 | 3.25 |
| 700 | 3.50 |

parts.content: Describe the trend shown by the data.
parts.markscheme:
- State the trend: As the wavelength increases, the fringe spacing increases. <marks>A1</marks>

1 mark total

steps.title: Identify the variables
steps.content:
To describe the trend, we first identify the two variables being compared in the table:
1. **Independent Variable**: Wavelength ($\lambda$) measured in nanometers ($\text{nm}$).
2. **Dependent Variable**: Fringe spacing ($s$) measured in millimeters ($\text{mm}$).

This experiment relates to **Topic C.3 Wave phenomena**, specifically double-slit interference.

steps.title: Inspect wavelength values
steps.content: Look at the values in the first column of the table. Notice that the wavelength starts at $450\text{ nm}$ and goes up to $700\text{ nm}$ in steps of $50\text{ nm}$. This shows that the wavelength is **increasing**.

steps.title: Inspect fringe spacing values
steps.content:
Now look at the corresponding values in the second column. As we move down the table from $450\text{ nm}$ to $700\text{ nm}$, the fringe spacing value changes from $2.25\text{ mm}$ to $3.50\text{ mm}$. 

We can see that these values are also **increasing** alongside the wavelength.

gdc.instructions: Calculate the difference between consecutive y-values.

ti84.instructions: Subtract the first spacing from the second, then the second from the third.

desmos.instructions: Perform simple subtraction to check the increment.

calculator.description: Verify the change in fringe spacing between the first few entries.

steps.title: State the trend
steps.content:
By comparing the two columns, we can conclude that there is a positive correlation. 

As the **wavelength increases**, the **fringe spacing increases**. 

This aligns with the double-slit formula from the Data Booklet:

$$\text{s} = \frac{\lambda D}{d}$$

Since $D$ and $d$ are constant, $s$ is directly proportional to $\lambda$.

steps.title: Observe the data pattern
steps.content: Let's start by looking at the relationship between the two columns in the table. We can see that as the values for wavelength ($\lambda$) increase from $450\text{ nm}$ to $700\text{ nm}$, the values for fringe spacing ($s$) also increase from $2.25\text{ mm}$ to $3.50\text{ mm}$.

steps.title: Link to the formula
steps.content:
To explain why this happens, we look at the double-slit formula provided in the **Waves** section of your IB Data Booklet:

$$s = \frac{\lambda D}{d}$$

This equation relates the fringe spacing ($s$), the wavelength ($\lambda$), the distance to the screen ($D$), and the slit separation ($d$).

steps.title: Identify constant variables
steps.content:
In this investigation, the student keeps the slit separation ($d$) and the screen distance ($D$) constant. Since $D$ and $d$ do not change, the term $\frac{D}{d}$ acts as a constant multiplier. 

This means that $s$ is directly proportional to $\lambda$ ($s \propto \lambda$). If you increase the wavelength, the fringe spacing must also increase to maintain this equality.

highlights.text: slit separation $d$ and screen distance $D$ constant

steps.title: Verify with calculation
steps.content:
We can verify this proportionality by checking if the ratio $\frac{s}{\lambda}$ is constant for the first and last data points.

For $450\text{ nm}$: $\frac{2.25}{450} = 0.005$
For $700\text{ nm}$: $\frac{3.50}{700} = 0.005$

Since the ratio is constant, it confirms a linear relationship.

gdc.instructions: Calculate 2.25 / 450 and 3.50 / 700 to check for a constant ratio.

ti84.instructions: Divide 2.25 by 450, then divide 3.50 by 700.

desmos.instructions: Enter the ratios to compare them.

calculator.description: Calculate the ratio of fringe spacing to wavelength to verify proportionality.

steps.title: State the final trend
steps.content:
Based on both the data provided and the theoretical equation, we can conclude the trend.

**Trend:** As the wavelength increases, the fringe spacing increases.

steps.title: Analyze the experimental data
steps.content: To describe the trend, we first look at the values in the table. We observe that as the **wavelength** $\lambda$ increases from $450\text{ nm}$ to $700\text{ nm}$, the **fringe spacing** $s$ also increases from $2.25\text{ mm}$ to $3.50\text{ mm}$. This suggests a positive correlation between the two variables.

steps.title: Calculate the constant ratio
steps.content:
Using the **Quantitative Ratio Analysis** approach, we calculate the ratio of fringe spacing to wavelength ($\frac{s}{\lambda}$) for multiple data points. This ratio helps determine if the relationship is linear and proportional.

From the **Waves** section of the data booklet, the formula for double-slit interference is:
$$s = \frac{\lambda D}{d}$$

If $D$ and $d$ are constant, then $\frac{s}{\lambda} = \frac{D}{d} = \text{constant}$. Let's test this with the data:

- For $\lambda = 450\text{ nm}$: $\frac{2.25}{450} = 0.005$
- For $\lambda = 600\text{ nm}$: $\frac{3.00}{600} = 0.005$
- For $\lambda = 700\text{ nm}$: $\frac{3.50}{700} = 0.005$

gdc.instructions: In the Run-Matrix menu, perform the divisions: 2.25 ÷ 450, 3.00 ÷ 600, and 3.50 ÷ 700.

ti84.instructions: Enter 2.25 / 450, then 3.00 / 600, and 3.50 / 700 on the home screen.

desmos.instructions: Type the ratios into the expression bars to compare the results.

calculator.description: Calculate the ratio s/λ for different data points to verify the proportional trend.

steps.title: Describe the final trend
steps.content:
Since the ratio $\frac{s}{\lambda}$ is constant ($0.005\text{ mm/nm}$) for all data points, we can conclude that the fringe spacing is **directly proportional** to the wavelength. 

In descriptive terms, as the **wavelength increases**, the **fringe spacing increases**. This trend is consistent with the wave model of light where longer wavelengths diffract more significantly, leading to wider spacing in the interference pattern.

highlights.text: As the wavelength increases, the fringe spacing increases.

parts.content: Explain, using wave interference, why a pattern of bright and dark fringes is formed.
parts.markscheme:
- Bright fringes form where waves from both slits arrive in phase (constructive interference). <marks>A1</marks>
- Dark fringes form where waves arrive out of phase by half a wavelength (destructive interference). <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Understanding Superposition
steps.content: To explain the pattern, we use the **Principle of Superposition**. When light waves from the two slits reach the same point on the screen, they overlap (superpose). The total displacement at any point is the sum of the displacements of the individual waves. This is a key concept in **Topic C.3: Wave phenomena**.

steps.title: Constructive Interference (Bright Fringes)
steps.content:
Bright fringes occur due to **constructive interference**. This happens when the waves from both slits arrive at the screen **in phase** (meaning their peaks align with peaks, and troughs with troughs). 

When waves are in phase, their amplitudes add together to produce a region of maximum intensity, which we see as a bright line.

steps.title: Destructive Interference (Dark Fringes)
steps.content:
Dark fringes occur due to **destructive interference**. This happens when the waves arrive at the screen **out of phase** by half a wavelength ($180^\circ$ or $\pi$ radians).

In this case, the peak of one wave meets the trough of the other. The displacements cancel each other out, resulting in a region of minimum intensity or darkness.

steps.title: Summary of Marks
steps.content:
To ensure full marks on this question, you must mention:
1.  **Bright fringes:** Waves arrive in phase, causing constructive interference.
2.  **Dark fringes:** Waves arrive out of phase (by half a wavelength), causing destructive interference.

steps.title: Understand the path difference
steps.content:
To explain the interference pattern, we first look at the geometry. When light leaves the two slits, it travels toward the screen. Because the slits are at different positions, the light from each slit usually travels a different distance to reach the same point on the screen. 

This difference in distance is known as the **path difference**. This concept is central to **Topic C.3: Wave phenomena**.

steps.title: Forming bright fringes
steps.content:
A **bright fringe** (maximum intensity) occurs when the light waves from both slits arrive at the screen **in phase**. This happens through **constructive interference**.

For the waves to be in phase, the path difference must be an integer multiple of the wavelength:
$$\text{Path Difference} = n\lambda$$
where $n = 0, 1, 2, \dots$

In this state, the peaks of one wave align with the peaks of the other, reinforcing the signal.

highlights.text: Bright fringes form where waves from both slits arrive in phase (constructive interference).

steps.title: Forming dark fringes
steps.content:
A **dark fringe** (minimum intensity) occurs when the light waves arrive at the screen exactly **out of phase** (anti-phase). This is known as **destructive interference**.

This happens when the path difference is a "half-integer" multiple of the wavelength:
$$\text{Path Difference} = (n + \frac{1}{2})\lambda$$
where $n = 0, 1, 2, \dots$

In this case, the peaks of one wave align with the troughs of the other, causing them to cancel each other out.

highlights.text: Dark fringes form where waves arrive out of phase by half a wavelength (destructive interference).

steps.title: Summary of the pattern
steps.content:
Because the path difference changes continuously as you move across the screen, the conditions for constructive and destructive interference alternate. 

This creates the characteristic pattern of equally spaced bright and dark fringes described by the double-slit formula in your data booklet:

$$\text{s} = \frac{\lambda D}{d}$$

steps.title: Convert Units to Meters
steps.content:
The question asks for the graph to be plotted against wavelength $\lambda$ in meters (m). Our table provides values in nanometers (nm). 

Since $1 \text{ nm} = 10^{-9} \text{ m}$, we convert each value:

$$\begin{aligned} 450 \text{ nm} &= 450 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m} = 4.50 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m} \\ 500 \text{ nm} &= 500 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m} = 5.00 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m} \\ 550 \text{ nm} &= 550 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m} = 5.50 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m} \\ 600 \text{ nm} &= 600 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m} = 6.00 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m} \\ 650 \text{ nm} &= 650 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m} = 6.50 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m} \\ 700 \text{ nm} &= 700 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m} = 7.00 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Type 450 * 10^-9 and press EXE.

ti84.instructions: Type 450 * 10^-9 and press ENTER.

calculator.description: Convert 450 nm to meters.

steps.title: Establish Linear Scales
steps.content:
To earn the first mark, we must label the axes with correct units and use a linear scale (evenly spaced increments).

- **y-axis**: Fringe spacing $s$ (mm). Range from $0$ to $4.0$ mm works well.
- **x-axis**: Wavelength $\lambda$ (m). Range from $0$ to $8.0 \times 10^{-7}$ m works well.

From the Data Booklet, we know the relationship for double-slit interference is:

$$s = \frac{\lambda D}{d}$$

Since $D$ and $d$ are constant, $s$ is directly proportional to $\lambda$, meaning the graph should be a straight line starting from the origin $(0,0)$.

steps.title: Plot Coordinates
steps.content:
Now, we accurately plot each data point from our table. Using the Point-by-Point Plotting method, we find the intersection of our $x$ and $y$ values:

- $(4.50 \times 10^{-7}, 2.25)$
- $(5.00 \times 10^{-7}, 2.50)$
- $(5.50 \times 10^{-7}, 2.75)$
- $(6.00 \times 10^{-7}, 3.00)$
- $(6.50 \times 10^{-7}, 3.25)$
- $(7.00 \times 10^{-7}, 3.50)$

Ensure each point is marked with a small cross ($+$ or $\times$).

steps.title: Draw Line of Best Fit
steps.content:
The final step is to draw a single, thin straight line using a ruler. 

Because the physics relationship $$s = \frac{\lambda D}{d}$$ predicts that if wavelength is zero, fringe spacing is zero, your line of best fit must pass through the **origin $(0,0)$**. 

Ensure the line passes as close as possible to all plotted points. This completes the requirements for the second mark.

highlights.text: line of best fit drawn through the origin

steps.title: Identify the relationship
steps.content:
To sketch the graph, we first look at the double-slit equation from the IB data booklet:

$$s = \frac{\lambda D}{d}$$

Since the slit separation $d$ and screen distance $D$ are kept constant, we can see that the fringe spacing $s$ is directly proportional to the wavelength $\lambda$ ($s \propto \lambda$). In mathematical terms, this follows the form $y = mx$, where the gradient $m = \frac{D}{d}$.

highlights.text: slit separation $d$ and screen distance $D$ constant

steps.title: Establish the origin point
steps.content:
Because $s$ is directly proportional to $\lambda$, if the wavelength were zero, the fringe spacing would also be zero. This means the line of best fit **must pass through the origin $(0,0)$**. 

We can verify this proportionality by checking the ratio $\frac{s}{\lambda}$ for the given data points:
$$\frac{2.25}{450} = 0.005 \text{ mm/nm}$$
$$\frac{3.50}{700} = 0.005 \text{ mm/nm}$$
Since the ratio is constant, the relationship is perfectly linear and starts at the origin.

steps.title: Label axes and units
steps.content:
When sketching the graph, the question specifies the units for each axis:
- **Vertical axis ($y$):** Fringe spacing $s$ in millimeters (mm).
- **Horizontal axis ($x$):** Wavelength $\lambda$ in meters (m).

To convert the table values for $\lambda$ to meters:
$$450 \text{ nm} = 450 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m} = 4.5 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}$$
$$700 \text{ nm} = 700 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m} = 7.0 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}$$

steps.title: Analyze the experimental variables
steps.content: In this experiment, the student is investigating how the fringe spacing ($s$) changes when the wavelength ($\\lambda$) is varied, while keeping the slit separation ($d$) and screen distance ($D$) constant. To find the relationship, we can use **Numerical Ratio Analysis** on the data provided in the table.

highlights.text: measures the distance between adjacent bright fringes (fringe spacing s )

steps.title: Calculate the numerical ratios
steps.content:
To determine if there is a direct proportionality, we calculate the ratio of fringe spacing to wavelength ($s/\\lambda$) for several data points from the table. 

$$\text{Ratio} = \frac{s}{\lambda}$$

Let's check three points:
1. For $\lambda = 450\text{ nm}$, $s = 2.25\text{ mm} \Rightarrow \frac{2.25}{450} = 0.005$
2. For $\lambda = 500\text{ nm}$, $s = 2.50\text{ mm} \Rightarrow \frac{2.50}{500} = 0.005$
3. For $\lambda = 700\text{ nm}$, $s = 3.50\text{ mm} \Rightarrow \frac{3.50}{700} = 0.005$

gdc.instructions: Perform the division s/λ for various pairs in the table.

ti84.instructions: Enter the division calculations on the home screen.

desmos.instructions: Calculate the ratios in the expression bar.

calculator.description: Calculate the ratio s/λ for different points in the table to check for constancy.

steps.title: Interpret the constant ratio
steps.content:
Since the ratio $\frac{s}{\lambda}$ is constant ($0.005\text{ mm/nm}$) for all values in the table, it indicates that $s$ is directly proportional to $\lambda$. This confirms the relationship provided in the **Waves** section of the IB data booklet:

$$s = \frac{\lambda D}{d}$$

If $D$ and $d$ are constant, then $s = (\text{constant}) \times \lambda$, which mathematically describes a straight line passing through the origin.

steps.title: State the graphical relationship
steps.content:
The constant ratio and the resulting linear trend mean that if you were to plot these points, you would get a straight line. Because the ratio is constant and doesn't change, the line must pass through the origin $(0,0)$. 

Therefore, the shape of the graph suggests that **fringe spacing is directly proportional to wavelength**.

steps.title: Analyze the rate of change
steps.content:
To understand the relationship between fringe spacing ($s$) and wavelength ($\lambda$), we first check if the rate of change (the slope) is constant. We can calculate the change in fringe spacing ($\Delta s$) divided by the change in wavelength ($\Delta \lambda$) for several intervals.

$$\text{Slope} = \frac{\Delta s}{\Delta \lambda}$$

For the first two data points:
$$\text{Slope} = \frac{2.50 - 2.25}{500 - 450} = \frac{0.25}{50} = 0.005 \text{ mm/nm}$$

For the last two data points:
$$\text{Slope} = \frac{3.50 - 3.25}{700 - 650} = \frac{0.25}{50} = 0.005 \text{ mm/nm}$$

Because the slope is constant, the relationship is **linear**.

gdc.instructions: Divide the difference in s-values by the difference in lambda-values for two intervals.

desmos.instructions: Calculate the slope ratios directly.

calculator.description: Calculate the slope between the first two points and the last two points.

steps.title: Check the intercept
steps.content:
A linear relationship follows the equation $s = m\lambda + c$. We can find the intercept ($c$) by substituting one of the points into this equation using our calculated slope ($m = 0.005$):

$$\begin{aligned} s &= 0.005 \lambda + c \\ 2.25 &= 0.005(450) + c \\ 2.25 &= 2.25 + c \\ c &= 0 \end{aligned}$$

Since the intercept is zero, the line passes through the origin $(0,0)$.

ti84.instructions: Multiply the slope by the wavelength: 0.005 * 450.

desmos.instructions: Multiply the slope by the wavelength: 0.005 * 450.

calculator.description: Verify the intercept by calculating 0.005 * 450.

steps.title: Determine the relationship
steps.content:
In physics, if a graph of two variables is a straight line that passes through the origin, we say the variables are **directly proportional**.

This matches the theoretical formula for double-slit interference found in your data booklet:

$$s = \frac{\lambda D}{d}$$

Since $D$ and $d$ are constant, $s \propto \lambda$.

steps.title: Confirm with the graph
steps.content:
If you were to plot these points, you would see a perfectly straight line pointing toward $(0,0)$. This confirms the relationship suggested by the data table.

**Final Conclusion:** The linear graph passing through the origin suggests that fringe spacing is **directly proportional** to wavelength.

steps.title: Identify the relevant formula
steps.content:
To understand the relationship between fringe spacing ($s$) and wavelength ($\lambda$), we start by looking at the double-slit interference formula provided in the **Waves** section of your data booklet:

$$s = \frac{\lambda D}{d}$$

steps.title: Analyze the constants
steps.content:
The question states that the student keeps the slit separation $d$ and screen distance $D$ constant. 

Because $D$ and $d$ do not change, the term $\frac{D}{d}$ acts as a constant, which we can call $k$. We can rewrite the formula as:

$$s = k \cdot \lambda$$

where $$k = \frac{D}{d}$$

highlights.text: slit separation $d$ and screen distance $D$ constant

steps.title: Link to linear graphs
steps.content:
In mathematics, an equation in the form $y = mx$ represents a straight line that passes through the origin $(0,0)$. 

Comparing our physics formula $s = k\lambda$ to the linear form $y = mx$:
- $y$ is the fringe spacing ($s$)
- $x$ is the wavelength ($\lambda$)
- $m$ (the gradient) is the constant $k$

If we plotted this data, we would get a straight line passing through the origin.

steps.title: Determine the relationship
steps.content:
A straight-line graph passing through the origin is the defining characteristic of a **direct proportionality**. 

Therefore, the shape of the graph suggests that the fringe spacing is directly proportional to the wavelength.

parts.content: The distance from the slits to the screen is $D=2.0 \mathrm{~m}$. Use the gradient of the graph to estimate the slit separation $d$. Use the relationship $s=\frac{\lambda D}{d}$.
parts.markscheme:
- Determine the gradient: $m = \frac{\Delta s}{\Delta \lambda} \approx 5.0 \times 10^{6} \mathrm{~mm} / \mathrm{m}$ (or $5000 \mathrm{~m/m}$) <marks>M1</marks>
- Calculate $d$ using $d = \frac{D \times 1000}{\text{gradient (mm/m)}}$ or equivalent unit conversion: <marks>A1</marks>

$$
d = \frac{2.0 \times 10^3}{5.0 \times 10^{6}} = 4.0 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~m} \quad (\text{or } 0.4 \mathrm{~mm})
$$

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify the linear relationship
steps.content:
To use the gradient of a graph, we first need to relate the given physics equation to the standard form of a straight line, $y = mx$. 

The question provides the double-slit formula:

$$s = \frac{\lambda D}{d}$$

If we plot fringe spacing ($s$) on the $y$-axis and wavelength ($\lambda$) on the $x$-axis, the equation becomes:

$$s = \left( \frac{D}{d} \right) \lambda$$

Here, the gradient ($m$) is equal to $\frac{D}{d}$.

steps.title: Calculate the gradient
steps.content:
We can calculate the gradient ($m$) by selecting two points from the table. Let's use the first and last points:
- Point 1: $(450\text{ nm}, 2.25\text{ mm})$
- Point 2: $(700\text{ nm}, 3.50\text{ mm})$

The gradient formula is:

$$m = \frac{\Delta s}{\Delta \lambda} = \frac{s_2 - s_1}{\lambda_2 - λ_1}$$

$$m = \frac{3.50 - 2.25}{700 - 450} = \frac{1.25\text{ mm}}{250\text{ nm}} = 0.005\text{ mm/nm}$$

gdc.instructions: Perform the division (3.50 - 2.25) / (700 - 450).

ti84.instructions: Enter the calculation (3.50 - 2.25) / (700 - 450) on the home screen.

desmos.instructions: Type the gradient formula directly to see the result.

calculator.description: Calculate the slope using two points from the table.

steps.title: Convert units to SI
steps.content:
To find $d$ in meters, we must convert the gradient into standard units (m/m). 

Recall that $1\text{ mm} = 10^{-3}\text{ m}$ and $1\text{ nm} = 10^{-9}\text{ m}$:

$$m = 0.005 \times \frac{10^{-3}\text{ m}}{10^{-9}\text{ m}}$$

$$m = 0.005 \times 10^6 = 5000$$

This value represents the change in fringe spacing per meter of wavelength.

steps.title: Solve for slit separation
steps.content:
Now we use the relationship between the gradient and the experimental constants derived in Step 0:

$$m = \frac{D}{d} \implies d = \frac{D}{m}$$

Given that the distance to the screen is $D = 2.0\text{ m}$:

$$d = \frac{2.0\text{ m}}{5000} = 4.0 \times 10^{-4}\text{ m}$$

In millimeters, this is $0.40\text{ mm}$.

gdc.instructions: Calculate 2.0 / 5000.

ti84.instructions: Enter 2.0 / 5000 on the home screen.

desmos.instructions: Divide 2 by the previously calculated slope m.

calculator.description: Divide the screen distance by the calculated gradient to find the slit separation.

steps.title: Identify the relationship
steps.content:
To find the slit separation $d$, we use the double-slit interference formula from the data booklet:

$$s = \frac{\lambda D}{d}$$

If we plot fringe spacing $s$ on the $y$-axis and wavelength $\lambda$ on the $x$-axis, the equation takes the linear form $y = mx$. Here, the gradient $m$ represents:

$$m = \frac{D}{d}$$

steps.title: Convert to SI units
steps.content:
Following the **SI Unit Conversion Approach**, we convert all values from the table into meters before calculating the gradient. This ensures our gradient is a dimensionless ratio ($m/m$).

- **Wavelength (nm to m):** Multiply by $10^{-9}$
- **Fringe Spacing (mm to m):** Multiply by $10^{-3}$

Let's pick the first and last data points:
- Point 1: $\lambda_1 = 450 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}$, $s_1 = 2.25 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$
- Point 2: $\lambda_2 = 700 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}$, $s_2 = 3.50 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$

steps.title: Calculate the gradient
steps.content:
Now we calculate the gradient $m$ using our SI values:

$$\begin{aligned} m &= \frac{\Delta s}{\Delta \lambda} \\ m &= \frac{(3.50 - 2.25) \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}}{(700 - 450) \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}} \\ m &= \frac{1.25 \times 10^{-3}}{250 \times 10^{-9}} \\ m &= 5000 \end{aligned}$$

This gradient represents the ratio $\frac{D}{d}$.

gdc.instructions: Calculate the change in y divided by change in x using the converted values.

desmos.instructions: Type the gradient formula directly into a cell.

calculator.description: Calculate the gradient using SI units.

steps.title: Solve for slit separation
steps.content:
Finally, we use the known distance to the screen $D = 2.0 \text{ m}$ to solve for $d$:

$$\begin{aligned} m &= \frac{D}{d} \\ d &= \frac{D}{m} \\ d &= \frac{2.0 \text{ m}}{5000} \\ d &= 0.0004 \text{ m} \end{aligned}$$

In scientific notation, this is $4.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}$ (or $0.40 \text{ mm}$).

gdc.instructions: Divide 2.0 by the previously calculated gradient.

ti84.instructions: Enter 2.0 / 5000 and press ENTER.

desmos.instructions: Enter the division to find the result in meters.

calculator.description: Divide the distance D by the gradient to find d.

steps.title: Identify the linear relationship
steps.content:
To find the slit separation $d$ using a graph, we first compare the given equation to the standard equation for a straight line, $y = mx + c$. 

Starting with the formula from the data booklet:
$$s = \frac{\lambda D}{d}$$

We can rearrange this to show how fringe spacing $s$ (our y-variable) depends on wavelength $\lambda$ (our x-variable):
$$s = \left( \frac{D}{d} \right) \lambda$$

Here, the **gradient ($m$)** of the line is equal to $\frac{D}{d}$. Since $D$ is a constant, finding the gradient allows us to solve for $d$.

*   **Wavelength $\lambda$ (x-values):** Convert $\text{nm}$ to $\text{m}$ by multiplying by $10^{-9}$.
*   **Fringe spacing $s$ (y-values):** Convert $\text{mm}$ to $\text{m}$ by multiplying by $10^{-3}$.

From the GDC, you will find that the relationship is perfectly linear ($r = 1$) with a gradient of:

steps.title: Calculate slit separation

Now we use the relationship between the gradient and the physical constants:
$$m = \frac{D}{d}$$

Rearranging to solve for $d$:

parts.content: Suggest one limitation in this experiment that could affect the precision of the fringe spacing measurements.
parts.markscheme:
Award **[2]** for one of the following limitations with explanation:
- Fringes may be blurry or hard to distinguish due to diffraction or ambient light. <marks>A1</marks> <marks>R1</marks>
- Measuring small distances with a ruler introduces significant percentage uncertainty. <marks>A1</marks> <marks>R1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify the measurement goal
steps.content:
In this experiment, the student is measuring the fringe spacing $s$, which is the distance between the centers of two adjacent bright fringes. According to the data booklet, this is governed by the formula:

$$s = \frac{\lambda D}{d}$$

To get a precise value for $s$, the student needs to identify exactly where the maximum intensity of each fringe occurs.

steps.title: Consider fringe clarity
steps.content:
In a real-world setup, bright fringes are not perfectly sharp, thin lines. Instead, they are broad regions of light. This is often caused by **diffraction** from the individual slits themselves or by **ambient light** in the room, which reduces the contrast between the bright and dark regions.

This falls under **Topic C.3 (Wave phenomena)**. If you recall the intensity patterns of double-slit interference, you'll remember that the fringes have a specific width rather than being infinitely narrow.

steps.title: Relate to measurement precision
steps.content:
Because the fringes are broad and potentially blurry, it becomes very difficult for the observer to pinpoint the exact **center** of the fringe. 

If you cannot identify the exact point of maximum intensity, your measurement of the distance $s$ will have a higher uncertainty. This lack of optical resolution directly limits how precise your fringe spacing data can be.

steps.title: Final Conclusion
steps.content:
The limitation is that the fringes may be blurry or hard to distinguish due to diffraction or ambient light. This affects precision because it makes it difficult to accurately locate the center of each fringe when measuring the spacing $s$.

According to the markscheme, stating the limitation and explaining why it makes measurement difficult earns the full 2 marks.

highlights.text: Fringes may be blurry or hard to distinguish due to diffraction or ambient light.

steps.title: Analyze the measurement scale
steps.content: To understand the limitations of this experiment, we first look at the magnitude of the values being measured. Looking at the data table, the fringe spacing $s$ ranges from approximately $2.25\text{ mm}$ to $3.50\text{ mm}$. These are very small absolute values for a manual measurement.

steps.title: Identify instrumental precision
steps.content: In a school laboratory, distance is typically measured using a standard millimeter ruler. The smallest division on such a ruler is $1\text{ mm}$, which gives an instrumental uncertainty of at least $\pm 0.5\text{ mm}$. In practice, this uncertainty might even be larger because the centers of the bright fringes can be difficult to pinpoint exactly.

steps.title: Evaluate percentage uncertainty
steps.content:
The impact of an instrument's precision depends on the scale of the measurement. We can calculate the percentage uncertainty using the formula:

$$\text{Percentage Uncertainty} = \frac{\text{Absolute Uncertainty}}{\text{Measured Value}} \times 100$$

For the smallest fringe spacing ($2.25\text{ mm}$), an uncertainty of $0.5\text{ mm}$ results in a percentage uncertainty of approximately $22.2\%$. Even for the largest spacing ($3.50\text{ mm}$), the uncertainty remains high at about $14.3\%$.

gdc.instructions:
1. Enter (0.5 / 2.25) * 100 into the calculator.
2. Enter (0.5 / 3.50) * 100 into the calculator.

ti84.instructions:
1. Divide 0.5 by 2.25 and multiply by 100.
2. Divide 0.5 by 3.50 and multiply by 100.

desmos.instructions: Type (0.5 / 2.25) * 100 and (0.5 / 3.50) * 100 in separate cells to find the percentage uncertainties.

calculator.description: Calculate the percentage uncertainty for the smallest and largest measured values to see the impact of ruler precision.

steps.title: State the final limitation
steps.content:
Because the fringe spacing values are so small, using a standard ruler introduces a very high percentage uncertainty. This significantly reduces the precision of the results and makes it difficult to establish a reliable relationship between $\lambda$ and $s$. 

To earn both marks, you must identify the limitation and explain its impact:
1. **Limitation:** Measuring small distances ($s$) with a ruler.
2. **Explanation:** This leads to a high percentage uncertainty in the data.

questions.specification: In a double-slit interference pattern, which change would cause the fringes to move closer together?

labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.3.2 Interference of waves
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question concerns the double-slit interference pattern and the relationship between fringe spacing, wavelength, slit separation, and screen distance, which is a fundamental concept in the interference of waves.

questions.specification: In a resonance tube experiment, a tuning fork with a fixed frequency $f = 500\ \text{Hz}$ is held above a vertical glass tube. The tube is slowly filled with water, and the lengths of the air column at which resonance occurs are measured. The first resonance is observed when the air column has a length $L_1 = 17.0\ \text{cm}$, and the second resonance is observed at $L_2 = 51.0\ \text{cm}$.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic C - Wave behaviour > C.4 Standing waves and resonance > C.4.1 Formation and properties of standing waves
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.4.1 Formation and properties of standing waves
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Parts (a) and (e) focus on how standing waves form in the air column via superposition of incident/reflected sound waves and the resonance condition.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic C - Wave behaviour > C.4 Standing waves and resonance > C.4.2 Standing wave patterns in systems
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.4.2 Standing wave patterns in systems
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Parts (b) and (d) require boundary conditions (node at water surface, antinode at open end) and drawing/identifying node–antinode displacement patterns for first and second resonances in a closed tube.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic C - Wave behaviour > C.4 Standing waves and resonance > C.4.3 Resonance in oscillatory systems
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.4.3 Resonance in oscillatory systems
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The task involves resonance in an oscillatory system (resonance tube), using successive resonant lengths (L1, L2) to determine wavelength/speed and discussing accuracy from using length differences.

parts.content: Describe how the interaction of sound waves within the air column leads to the formation of a standing wave.
parts.markscheme:
- The sound waves produced by the tuning fork travel down the tube and reflect at the water surface <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The incident and reflected waves overlap and interfere (superpose), creating a standing wave pattern with stationary nodes and antinodes <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: State the boundary condition that must be met at the water surface for the air column to resonate.
parts.markscheme: - There must be a displacement node at the water surface <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Calculate the wavelength of the sound waves produced by the tuning fork.
parts.markscheme:
- Recognize that the distance between the first and second resonance positions corresponds to half a wavelength: $\dfrac{\lambda}{2} = L_2 - L_1$ <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Calculate $\lambda = 2 \times (0.510 - 0.170) = 0.680\ \text{m}$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Sketch the displacement standing wave patterns for the air column at the first and second resonant lengths. Label the nodes and antinodes.
parts.markscheme:
- First resonance: Sketch showing a node at the water surface and an antinode at the open end <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Second resonance: Sketch showing a node at the water surface, one intermediate node/antinode pair, and an antinode at the open end <marks>1 mark</marks>

![Resonance Patterns](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/ramen-ramen-ib-chemistry-new-fcfe784e-afd2-443f-b7fe-e07d8054e4ff-1771938518510/3fd1a916-b035-464c-819a-44a991a672b3.jpeg)

parts.content: Determine the speed of sound in air from these measurements and explain why using the difference $(L_2 - L_1)$ is typically more accurate than using $4 \times L_1$ to find the wavelength.
parts.markscheme:
- Calculate speed $v = f\lambda = 500 \times 0.680 = 340\ \text{m/s}$ <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Explain that the antinode does not occur exactly at the physical end of the tube (end correction) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- State that subtracting $L_1$ from $L_2$ cancels out the end correction constant, eliminating systematic error <marks>1 mark</marks>
